title: Period After Reverting? A Gentle Guide to Ḥayḍ, Prayer Pauses & Staying Close to Allah
text:
Photo by Jaroslava Petrášová on Unsplash
If you just became Muslim and your period started, you might feel confused, disappointed, or even afraid you’re “doing something wrong.” You’re not.
In Islam, menstruation (ḥayḍ) is a normal body state Allah created, and Allah also gave a law of mercy for it: some acts of worship pause, while many other doors stay wide open.

So you embraced Islam, and now your period has started.
A lot can rise up at once: Am I allowed to pray? Can I touch the Qur’an? Does this mean I’m “impure”? How do I stay close to Allah right now?
These questions are normal, especially if ṣalāh has just become your anchor. When that anchor temporarily pauses, many new Muslim women feel sadness or panic.
Let this be clear from the beginning: menstruation in Islam is not a punishment, not a sign of lesser worth, and not a stain on your dignity. Your relationship with Allah does not stop when your period starts. The form of worship changes. but worship itself continues.

Menstruation in Islam: what it means (and what it does not)
Allah says:

They ask you about menstruation. Say: it is a discomfort… (al‑Baqarah 2:222)
The Qur’an speaks realistically. Menstruation is acknowledged as a bodily state that can involve discomfort and vulnerability, and it comes with rulings. Islam does not deny biology, and it does not shame it.
A key distinction will protect you emotionally:
Menstruation is a ritual state, not a moral failure. Sin is a matter of disobedience and harm; ritual states are conditions Allah created that change certain acts of worship for a time. So when you’re menstruating, nothing is “wrong” with your faith, your worth, or your sincerity. You are simply in a state Allah built into the female body and legislated for with mercy.

The basic ruling, simply
In mainstream Islamic law, menstruating women do not perform the formal ritual prayer (ṣalāh) until bleeding ends and purification (ghusl) is completed.
This comes from the teaching of the Prophet ﷺ. In well-known narrations, ʿĀ’ishah (raḍiyAllāhu ʿanhā) explains that women did not make up prayers missed during menstruation, while missed fasting in Ramaḍān is made up later.
Beginner note: this is a general guide, not a personal fatwā. If you follow a specific madhhab or trusted teacher, follow that guidance for details.

“But prayer is important, why would it be paused?”
Because prayer is central, the pause can feel emotionally heavy.
But hold this firmly: Allah who commanded ṣalāh is the same Allah who exempted you from ṣalāh in this state. The exemption itself is from Him.
Sometimes worship looks like performing a command. Sometimes worship looks like accepting a concession with trust. Both are obedience.
And the mercy becomes obvious when you picture the alternative: imagine being required to make up years of daily prayers each month. Allah did not burden women with that hardship.

A systems view: what may be happening in the body
Menstruation isn’t a random inconvenience. It’s a coordinated biological process involving hormones, uterine shedding, inflammation signaling, fluid shifts, energy demands, and nervous-system effects.
For some people it’s light; for others it can reshape the whole inner climate: energy dips, pain, brain fog, lower mood, irritability, sleep disruption. Islam’s law is realistic about this. It doesn’t demand identical outward performance from people in different physical states. The law aligns with human design.

You are not “filthy”
Many women carry cultural shame around periods and are told they’re dirty, cursed, or spiritually inferior. Islam rejects that framing.
In the Prophet’s ﷺ household, menstruation was treated as a normal part of life, with dignity, tenderness, and clarity. The rulings are real, but the tone is mercy, not humiliation.

If I can’t pray, how do I worship now?
This is where Islam becomes unexpectedly spacious.
Ṣalāh is a magnificent form of worship, but worship is larger than one ritual. Closeness to Allah can be carried by remembrance, supplication, learning, patience, service, repentance, gratitude, and good character.
So rather than thinking, “I’m blocked,” you can think, “Allah redirected my worship.”

A gentle worship rhythm for menstruation
If you miss the structure of prayer, give your day a few soft “return points.”
Let dhikr become your background thread: quiet remembrance throughout the day, in bed, on a walk, while making tea. Let duʿā become your direct line: speak to Allah in your own words, for guidance, healing, steadiness, relief, protection, and closeness.
Let learning be your anchor when energy is low: a short lesson on purification, a few pages of sīrah, a reflection on Allah’s names, or a reliable women’s fiqh class. And let character be your worship in motion: guarding the tongue, being gentle under strain, fulfilling trusts, serving people with sincerity.
If you want a simple practice that feels surprisingly stabilizing: when each prayer time enters, pause for two minutes, not to “perform,” but to return. Sit, breathe, remember Allah, make duʿā, or read a short beneficial passage. You are telling your heart: Allah is still the reference.
Practical: a “period worship menu” (pick 3)
Choose a few that match your energy level. Consistency matters more than quantity.
Morning intention (30 seconds): “O Allah, I accept Your mercy-law today. Make this time worship.”
Dhikr anchor (2–5 min): Astaghfirullāh (seeking forgiveness), SubḥānAllāh, Alḥamdulillāh, Allāhu akbar, and Lā ilāha illaAllāh.
Duʿā list (5 min): Write 5 personal duʿās you can repeat all week (guidance, healing, steadiness, protection, barakah, good character).
One page of knowledge (5–15 min): purification basics, women’s fiqh basics, sīrah, or one of Allah’s names.
Listening Qur’an (10–20 min): recitation while resting, commuting, or doing light chores.
Salah-time “return pause” (2 min): when each prayer time enters, sit and return to Allah with duʿā and dhikr (no ritual prayer).
Sadaqah / service micro‑act (5–15 min): one small act for someone else (see ideas below).
Gratitude reset (2 min): list 3 gifts, then ask Allah to increase you in gratitude.
Practical: what you can still do (and do beautifully)
Even though ṣalāh pauses, these doors stay open:
Dhikr and duʿā (at any time, in any language).
Seeking knowledge (reading, listening, classes).
Listening to Qur’an, and engaging Qur’an through translation/tafsīr (follow your teacher’s guidance on touching/reading Arabic text).
Good character under strain: patience, gentleness, guarding the tongue.
Service and charity: helping people, making things easier for others.
Self‑care as amanah: rest, nutrition, pain care, so you can return to worship strong.
Practical: “service to Allah” ideas (low energy → high energy)
Pick what’s realistic. Small sincere acts can outweigh big inconsistent ones.
Low energy: send a sincere duʿā message to someone; forgive someone privately; make tea for a family member/roommate; tidy one small corner.
Medium energy: cook or prep something and share; check in on someone lonely; run one errand that removes burden from another.
Higher energy: volunteer locally; support a sister/new revert; give a small class note summary to someone who’s learning.
Practical: simple resources (keep it stable)
Because there are differences between schools on some details, the safest approach is:

pick one reliable teacher/school, 

learn the basics, 

 don’t hop between opinions when you’re emotionally raw.
A starter list to search for (use these exact phrases):
“Hayd and nifas fiqh for beginners”
“What worship is allowed during menstruation”
“Do you make up prayers after menstruation”
“Can you read Qur’an during menstruation (madhhab)”
If you want a gentle learning path, search for:
“women’s fiqh course purification”
“Riyad as‑Salihin dhikr and dua” (short chapters are easy to digest)
“Hisn al‑Muslim (Fortress of the Muslim) PDF” (a classic duʿā collection)
Practical: a tiny “period plan” template
You can copy/paste this into your notes each day:
Today’s energy: low / medium / high
3 worship acts (pick): dhikr | duʿā | knowledge | Qur’an listening | service | gratitude
One person to serve: _
One thing to let go of: _
One duʿā I will repeat all day: _

The Qur’an during menstruation (gentle clarity)
There are scholarly differences about details of touching/reading the Arabic muṣḥaf during menstruation. If you’re new, don’t let that become an identity crisis.
Choose one trusted teacher or school and be consistent.
In the meantime, many women keep their Qur’an connection alive through listening to recitation, reading translation, reading tafsīr, and studying meanings and notes. (Use apps as your teacher advises.) The goal is not to “pause Qur’an entirely,” but to keep the heart connected in the form that’s available.

If you feel sad you can’t pray
That sadness can come from love of worship, it isn’t automatically a bad sign.
But don’t let sadness turn into false beliefs. Not praying right now does not mean Allah is displeased with you, that you’re spiritually blocked, or that you’re failing. It means Allah placed you in a state He created, and He gave you a different mode of worship today.

When can you start praying again? (and how to do ghusl)
This is one of the most common questions — and it’s good to get it clear so you don’t stay in limbo.
1. First: be sure bleeding has actually ended
In general, you can return to prayer once you are confident your period has finished. That means:
No more menstrual blood
Many women will also see either complete dryness or a clear/white discharge that signals the end (depending on what your normal cycle looks like)
If you’re new to this, don’t overthink it on day one. The safest approach is to learn the “end sign” that matches your body and then stick with one consistent method.
Beginner note: there are differences in details between madhhabs (for example, about maximum/minimum days, and how to treat brown discharge/spotting). If you follow a specific school or trusted teacher, follow that.
2. As soon as you’re sure it ended: do ghusl (the full-body purification wash)
Ghusl is what returns you to the state needed for ṣalāh.
You don’t have to make it complicated. The essentials are simple:
Intention (niyyah): in your heart, intend to purify yourself for worship.
Water reaches the whole body: make sure water touches all of your skin and hair roots/scalp as best as you reasonably can.
A commonly taught step-by-step ghusl (simple + practical) is:

Say bismillāh (quietly is fine).

Wash your hands.

Wash the private area and remove any traces of blood.

Make wuḍū’ (ablution) like you would for prayer. (Some people delay washing the feet until the end if standing in water, either is fine.)

Pour water over your head and rub so water reaches the scalp.

Wash the entire body, ensuring water reaches everywhere: underarms, between fingers/toes, behind knees, and any skin folds.
If your hair is braided or difficult to fully undo, scholars differ on what is required, so again, follow your teacher/school. The goal is not perfectionism; the goal is valid purification.
3. Then: return to ṣalāh at the next prayer time
Once you’ve done ghusl, you can pray again.
If you finish ghusl during a prayer time, most scholars say you should pray that prayer within its time.
If a prayer time passed while you were still menstruating, you do not make up those missed prayers.
Don’t wait to “feel spiritual.” Just return. Often the feeling comes after obedience.

Your body does not interrupt your worth before Allah.
He created your biology. He legislated for your reality. He knows your fatigue, your emotions, your discomfort, and your longing to worship.
If you turn to Him in the form available to you, you are not behind.
All benefit is from Allah. Any mistakes are from me.
Alḥamdulillāhi Rabbil‑ʿālamīn.

title: Beyond Marriage: A Woman’s Other Noble Paths in Islam
text:
Intent: This is a permitted and historically witnessed path for some Muslim women, not a prescription for all. In Islam, worth is measured by taqwā (God-consciousness), not by marital status.
In many Muslim communities, marriage is presented as the central life path for women, almost a natural destination: build a family, raise children, create a home, and fulfill an honored role. There is truth in that. Marriage in Islam is noble, protective, and deeply meaningful when approached with sincerity, mercy, and responsibility.
But it is not the only honorable path.
Islamic history, when examined carefully rather than selectively, shows a wider landscape. Alongside wives and mothers, there were also women whose lives were centered primarily on knowledge, worship, teaching, charity, scholarship, public benefit, and spiritual refinement. Some married and later became teachers. Some never married. Some were widowed or divorced and devoted themselves to service. Some supported institutions of learning. Some became authorities consulted by men and women alike.
This is not an argument against marriage. Nor is it an attempt to import modern individualism into Islam. It is a reminder of something older: within the boundaries of the Qur’an and Sunnah, there has long been more than one dignified form of female flourishing.

The foundational principle: human worth is not reduced to marital status
The Qur’an repeatedly grounds human worth in taqwā, faith, righteous action, patience, truthfulness, humility, generosity, and remembrance of Allah, not in whether a person is married.
Allah says:

Indeed, the Muslim men and Muslim women, the believing men and believing women, the obedient men and obedient women, the truthful men and truthful women, the patient men and patient women... the men who remember Allah often and the women who do so, for them Allah has prepared forgiveness and a great reward. (Qur’an 33:35)
This verse is notable because it lists spiritual and moral qualities in parallel for men and women. The axis is not marital identity. The axis is devotion and character.
Likewise:

I will not allow the work of any worker among you to be lost, whether male or female; you are of one another. (Qur’an 3:195)
And:

Whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, while being a believer. We will surely grant them a good life. (Qur’an 16:97)
These verses do not deny the value of marriage. They place it in proportion: marriage can be a vehicle for worship, but it is not the sole measure of a woman’s purpose.

Marriage in Islam: honored, but not universalized into obligation
The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ encouraged marriage in many narrations and established it as a blessed institution rooted in mercy, chastity, companionship, and mutual rights.
But encouragement is not identical to a one-size-fits-all command for every person in every circumstance.
Islamic law recognizes differences in capacity, desire, circumstance, obligations, health, temperament, and calling. Scholars discussed marriage through nuanced legal categories depending on the person’s condition: recommended for some, necessary for some, neutral for others, even discouraged if one would fail rights or cause harm.
That nuance matters.
Some people are drawn strongly to family life. Others are drawn to scholarship, caretaking, public service, or sustained devotional discipline. Some want both and balance both. Islam historically contained these variations.

Why a woman might choose a different path
Not every life choice is a manifesto. Sometimes it is simply qadar: the right match does not come; a woman is widowed; responsibilities shift; health requires a certain structure.
But a woman may also sincerely choose a life not centered on marriage for legitimate reasons:
1. A strong calling to knowledge
She may feel pulled toward studying Qur’an, hadith, law, theology, language, medicine, psychology, or teaching. Serious scholarship requires time, discipline, and continuity.
2. A desire for service
She may feel called to care for the poor, educate children, support communities, heal the sick, build institutions, or organize charity.
3. Spiritual temperament
Some people are inwardly drawn to worship, contemplation, dhikr, self-discipline, and a quieter life organized around Allah.
4. Realistic self-knowledge
She may know that marriage, in her current state or life structure, would divide her energy in ways that are unwise.
5. Circumstance
Not everyone finds a suitable spouse. Islam does not teach that such a person is unfinished or lesser.
6. Refusal to make marriage an idol
Sometimes marriage becomes socially inflated into proof of worth. A believer may consciously reject that distortion while still honoring marriage itself.

Historical women who embodied broader possibilities
ʿĀʾisha bint Abī Bakr (ra): scholar, teacher, legal mind
ʿĀʾisha (ra), wife of the Prophet ﷺ, is among the greatest transmitters of hadith and one of the most important intellectual figures in Islam.
After the Prophet’s death, people traveled to learn from her. She gave legal judgments, corrected misunderstandings, transmitted prophetic knowledge, and was known for sharp intelligence and precision.
Her life shows something crucial: even within marriage, a woman’s role can extend far beyond domestic reduction. She became part of the intellectual architecture of Islam itself.
Why this matters today: it breaks the false binary that a woman must choose between being honored in family life or being intellectually significant. She was both.

Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya: devotion as center
Rābiʿa became known for radical sincerity in worship and detachment from status. She is remembered in the spiritual tradition as someone whose life was organized around love of Allah, ascetic discipline, and inward purification.
Whether every anecdote about her is historically certain is secondary to the broader truth: Muslim memory preserved her as a model of a woman whose center was God rather than worldly rank.
Why this matters today: it represents a life where devotion is not decorative — it is structural.

Karīma al-Marwaziyya: female authority in hadith
Karīma al-Marwaziyya was a highly respected transmitter of Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī. Scholars studied under her and valued her reliability.
This matters because it shows that female scholarly authority was not alien to the tradition. It existed within it.
Why this matters today: it undermines the myth that serious textual scholarship belonged only to men.

Fāṭima al-Fihri: builder of learning
Fāṭima al-Fihri is traditionally associated with founding al-Qarawiyyīn in Fez, one of the world’s oldest continuously operating institutions of learning.
Whether described as university, mosque-school, or educational complex, the point remains: a Muslim woman’s legacy can be institution-building.
Why this matters today: service to the ummah can include creating structures that outlive you.

The many muhaddithāt (female hadith scholars)
Beyond famous names, Islamic history includes numerous women who taught hadith, granted ijāzah, transmitted texts, and were sought out by scholars.
Some modern researchers have documented long chains of female scholarship often forgotten in simplified storytelling.
Why this matters today: the true historical picture is broader than many communities currently remember.

Was this more common in earlier times?
In some respects, yes, not because “women were freer” in a simplistic sense, nor because history was uniformly ideal. Many women also faced hardship, patriarchy, economic dependence, and social limits.
But earlier Muslim societies often had stronger recognition of knowledge as a sacred vocation. Religious learning was socially meaningful. Endowments supported scholars. Transmission networks existed. A person could devote themselves to learning and teaching in ways modern consumer society often does not reward.
Today, many communities overemphasize marriage as the singular success marker while underinvesting in scholarship, intellectual formation, and disciplined service. That imbalance can make the historical range harder to see.

Important clarifications
This is not a rejection of family life
Motherhood and marriage remain among the most honorable human responsibilities when lived well. Many great women scholars were also wives and mothers.
This is not license for egoistic isolation
A “different path” is not code for self-centeredness, contempt for others, or refusal of responsibility.
This is not romantic escapism
A life of knowledge and worship can involve loneliness, sacrifice, obscurity, financial limits, and discipline. It is not an aesthetic identity.

What the Qur’anic balance looks like (questions that keep the path honest)

Is this drawing me closer to Allah or just away from discomfort?

Am I choosing service or merely avoiding vulnerability?

Is there discipline in my life, or only preference?

Am I benefiting others?

Am I maintaining chastity, character, and sincerity?

Am I open to what Allah may bring later, or attached to an identity?

For the woman who feels called differently
If you do not feel that marriage should be the center of your life, you are not automatically outside the tradition.
You may be called to become learned. You may be called to heal others. You may be called to teach. You may be called to build something beneficial. You may be called to a quieter life of worship and integrity. You may later marry, or not.
The measure is not conformity to social pressure.
The measure is whether life is aligned with Allah through truthfulness, discipline, benefit, and sincere action.

Islam did not give women only one script.
It gave principles, responsibilities, boundaries, and pathways to excellence.
Marriage is one noble path. It is not the only noble path.
For some women, the deepest contribution may come through family life. For others, through knowledge. For others, through service. For others, through a combination known only over time.
What matters is not performing a role. What matters is walking a life that is real, responsible, and pleasing to Allah.

title: The World Isn’t Ending. It’s Becoming Brittle.
text:
A systems view of 2026: not apocalypse, buffer failure.

People keep asking some version of: Is the world collapsing?
They want a clean answer. A single headline. A yes or no.
Here’s the honest answer: the world system in 2026 is not “dead.” But it is becoming less buffered, more shock‑sensitive, and more unequal in who absorbs damage. That matters more than any single crisis.
“Collapse” is the wrong mental model, not because things are fine, but because it suggests a dramatic, uniform, movie‑style ending. What we’re actually living through is slower, more mundane, and more dangerous: buffer failure.
When buffers erode, you don’t need an apocalypse to get suffering. You just need normal life plus a few shocks. And shocks are no longer rare.

The real story isn’t one crisis. It’s the interaction term.
If you look at climate alone, you’ll miss it. If you look at geopolitics alone, you’ll miss it. If you look at mental health alone, you’ll miss it.
The defining feature of this era isn’t any one variable, it’s the way they reinforce each other.
A hotter climate raises baseline volatility, which strains food systems, water systems, and infrastructure. Geopolitical rivalry reduces cooperation at the exact moment cooperation is most needed, while displacement increases pressure on borders, housing, and social cohesion. Health systems remain uneven, mental‑health needs outstrip capacity, and budgets harden around “security” while resilience stays underbuilt.
So the world feels unstable because the system is now structured to be unstable. Not by intention. By structure.

Climate stress isn’t “future.” It’s background.
Climate isn’t a distant scenario anymore. It’s a permanent pressure gradient acting on everything: food, water, migration, infrastructure, insurance, politics.
This is what people still don’t want to emotionally accept: the baseline has shifted.
In a stable system, extremes are occasional. In this system, extremes are a recurring input. And when recurring extremes collide with aging infrastructure, fragile supply chains, political distrust, and rising inequality, recovery often can’t complete before the next hit arrives.

Geopolitics is hardening, and resilience loses the budget fight
The global system is reprioritizing hard power.
This matters because budgets are moral documents. They reveal purpose.
When states are anxious, they fund defense, surveillance, and border control. They do not fund long‑horizon maintenance: prevention, adaptation, mental health, community stability, trust‑building institutions.
So we are building a world that can respond with force, but struggles to respond with care. That is a recipe for chronic instability.

The humanitarian system is already overloaded
If your model of “collapse” requires things to look like Mad Max everywhere, you’ll miss the real signal.
The signal is simpler and more diagnostic: the humanitarian system is routinely overwhelmed, and funding constraints disrupt basic services.
When conflict, climate shocks, and underfunding interact, you don’t get isolated emergencies. You get a new normal of interruption, clinics closing, food insecurity persisting, displacement compounding.
A system that can’t meet basic needs at the margins eventually brings the margins inward.

Physical health didn’t collapse, but it stratified
Global health didn’t “collapse,” but the trajectory is not comforting. Recent years showed how quickly progress can reverse, and how uneven recovery is.
Here’s the part we avoid saying plainly: we built a world where longevity is not a shared outcome, it’s a gated one.
Health is increasingly dependent on state capacity, personal wealth, and geopolitical luck. That isn’t progress. That’s stratification.

Mental health: the ignored structural load‑bearing crisis
Mental health isn’t a “side issue.” It’s load‑bearing.
A society can’t run on chronic dysregulation, individual or collective. When mental‑health capacity is underbuilt, you don’t just get more suffering. You get system‑level consequences: trust drops, burnout rises, attention fragments, addiction and escapism become more common, family strain increases, and politics becomes more volatile and punitive.
Then people act surprised when communities fracture.
The system is asking human beings to function like machines while starving them of what sustains human functioning.

Children and nutrition: degrading the future while promising prosperity
This is one of the most obscene contradictions of modern life: governments talk about “the future” while letting childhood conditions deteriorate for hundreds of millions.
When childhood nutrition and stability are hit, the effects compound across decades, cognition, health, behavior, education, earnings, family formation, social cohesion.
A system that degrades childhood while promising prosperity is eating its own seed corn.

The real problem isn’t events. It’s structure.
This is where Donella Meadows becomes painfully relevant.
She taught that systems generate their own characteristic behavior, and that the least visible parts matter most: interconnections and purpose.
Most governments manage events. They don’t repair stocks.
So the world repeats a familiar loop. A heatwave triggers an emergency response. Migration spikes trigger crackdowns. Mental health crises trigger waiting‑list patches. Inflation shocks trigger temporary subsidies. Food shocks trigger short‑term relief.
All of that can be necessary. But it’s still event management.
Meanwhile the underlying stocks degrade: trust, ecological stability, infrastructure quality, public health capacity, institutional legitimacy, household resilience.
When stocks erode, the system becomes brittle even during “quiet” periods.

Delays are killing us, because politics can’t handle them
The system is full of delays. Emissions become warming, warming becomes infrastructure failure, failure becomes migration, migration becomes conflict. Childhood deprivation becomes adult instability. Chronic stress becomes disease, disease reduces capacity, reduced capacity becomes poverty, and poverty amplifies stress.
But political cycles reward short horizons. So we get a repeating pattern: underreact early, panic late, overcorrect politically, then forget.
That’s not just a moral failure. It’s a systems failure.

So what’s the trajectory?
Not “the world ends tomorrow.”
Something more sober, and in some ways more terrifying, because it’s survivable enough to normalize.
The most likely near‑term future is not competent global salvation or instant collapse, but uneven adaptation under rising stress. Some countries and cities will do better than others. Emergency spending will rise. Resilience rhetoric will increase. The green transition will keep moving, but partially and unevenly. Living costs will climb in stressed sectors. Some states will respond with more surveillance and control. And inequality will become more visible in who is protected, and who is left exposed.
If I compress it into one sentence: the world system in 2026 is not dead, but it is becoming more brittle, more militarized, more unequal, and less forgiving of bad governance.

The brutal inference: what the system is for
Meadows says you can infer a system’s purpose from its behavior.
So let’s stop pretending.
If you infer purpose from behavior, then many governments are orienting society around economic throughput, strategic competition, supply security, short‑term legitimacy management, and the protection of incumbent institutions, around social order more than social healing.
Not primarily around long‑term resilience, human flourishing, ecological regeneration, preventive health, or community repair.
That’s why everything feels “busy” but not corrective. The machine is running. It’s just running toward brittleness.

Bottom line
The real problem isn’t that we have “issues.”
The real problem is that we are running growth‑and‑security systems inside physical limits, with weakened cooperation, eroding trust, and underfunded human maintenance.
We’re not heading into one neat collapse. We’re heading into a world where stability is expensive and uneven, where shocks are normal, and where the people without buffers pay first.
And unless the system’s purpose changes, unless we rebuild buffers and invest in human and ecological maintenance, this era won’t feel like a crisis.
It will feel like a permanent condition.
If you want a single closing line:
The future won’t announce itself as collapse. It will arrive as “normal life,” with no margin left.

Honestly? Your skepticism is understandable.
When we look at the current state of the world, a full, coherent, timely reorientation by governments looks unlikely in the near term. Not impossible, but unlikely.
It feels unlikely because incentives are still misaligned: too many systems reward short‑term GDP growth, electoral wins, optics, crisis‑management theater, and the protection of incumbents. It also feels unlikely because fragmentation is rising, countries are competing over energy, supply chains, migration control, military position, and technological advantage, and competition makes coordinated action harder.
Add the trust problem: public trust in institutions is weak in many places, and without trust even good policy faces resistance. Then add the compounding problem: climate, housing, demographics, mental health, inequality, debt, and ecological strain interact. Reform tends to be sector‑by‑sector and slower than the problem network. And finally, many leaders govern reactively. They respond after visible pain, not before structural risk, so adaptation arrives late and expensive.
But “unlikely” is not the same as “hopeless.” Systems can still change through shocks, local innovation, technological shifts, generational replacement, fiscal necessity, citizen pressure, competent municipal and regional leadership, and market repricing of risk. History is full of late pivots.
The problem is that late pivots are usually messier than early ones.
So what does this mean personally? Don’t base your life on waiting for governments to become wise. Build resilience at the scale you can influence: your health, your skills, your savings, your community, your psychological stability, your adaptability, and your local networks.
My direct read is simple. If you ask, “Will governments collectively solve this cleanly and in time?” the answer is: highly unlikely. If you ask, “Can meaningful adaptation still happen in uneven, imperfect ways?” the answer is: yes.
When the world becomes noisy, unstable, and morally confused, orientation to the Creator is not less relevant, it becomes more necessary. In my next article I will write about it.

title: When My Image of God Broke
text:
I did not convert to Islam for identity.
Not for aesthetics. Not for belonging. Not because I wanted a ready-made personality. Not because I needed a tribe to absorb me.
I converted because the Qur'an described patterns I had already seen.
For more than ten years I had been studying systems, psychology, trauma, regulation, feedback loops, cognition, and power. I had watched, in contemplation, how the self constructs itself from echoes: sensation arises, attention locates it, meaning layers onto it, and suddenly there is a "me" that feels sovereign but is actually process, state-dependent, context-dependent, unreliable.
I had seen that entropy is not only physical. It appears psychologically and socially. Any system without external regulation degrades. Any mind without orientation drifts. Any organism without boundaries destabilizes.
So when I encountered the Qur'an speaking about remembrance, discipline, justice, limits, sincerity, and guidance, it did not feel alien. It felt like another language for something real.
Even al-Fātiḥah, the seven-verse opening, struck me as calibration rather than petition. Ihdinā al-ṣirāṭ al-mustaqīm, not "give us what we want," but "orient us." Not "remove difficulty," but "keep us aligned." That resonated with what I knew about self-regulation: the system does not need comfort. It needs correct reference.
On February 8th I said the shahada. Not because I was looking for costume religion. Because the map was accurate.

Then Everything Broke
Later I read verses about marriage, gender roles, social law, inheritance, and the 7th-century container in which revelation emerged.
And I erupted.
Not politely. Not academically. My throat closed. My chest tightened. I felt rage, disgust, betrayal, confusion. I wanted to throw the book across the room.
How could this be divine guidance for my life now? I am a woman in 2026. I work as a nurse. I carry trauma. I live in a world completely unlike ancient Arabia. I was not reading from a neutral chair in a library. I was reading through a body marked by abuse, distrust, and violated authority.
So I reacted.
But eventually I saw something deeper. I was not only reacting to the text. I was reacting to authority itself, to men who harmed me, to systems that failed me, to the expectation that I should trust what I had learned not to trust. The verses were a trigger, but the ammunition was already loaded.
And beneath even that was something more hidden.

The Real Collapse
I already knew, intellectually, that God is not a human being. I knew the theological claim: unlike creation, beyond image, beyond comparison.
But emotionally, I was still expecting God to behave like a person.
I wanted proof the way a person gives proof. Comfort the way a parent gives comfort. Fairness the way a caretaker gives fairness. Answers the way a teacher gives answers. I wanted God to remove burden from my shoulders, to make sense on my terms, to be relatable in human categories.
That image broke.
And the break was grief. Not because God had abandoned me, but because the God I had constructed, the projected parent, the manageable deity, the entity who owed me explanation, had never existed. I was mourning a shelter I had built myself.
This is why people defend projections so fiercely. They are not only defending doctrine. They are defending psychological architecture. When the architecture collapses, the exposure is raw.

What Changed
I do not possess certainty now. I still do not fully understand what revelation is, direct speech, mediated encounter, inspired wisdom expressed through history, or something layered and mixed. I hold these possibilities without forcing a verdict.
But I now see that demanding God appear in forms my mind can comfortably grasp may itself be the fundamental error. The problem was not only that revelation was difficult. The problem was that I wanted ultimate reality to shrink into categories built for human relationships.

Guidance Reconsidered
I kept asking: Where is the guidance? I wanted customized instructions. Immediate clarity. Rescue.
But the Fātiḥa does not ask for rescue. It asks for orientation. A compass, not a chauffeur. A path, not teleportation. A mirror, not sedation.
I now understand that guidance can appear as:

conscience that refuses to settle for less than truth

reality feedback that corrects false assumptions

repeated correction until the lesson integrates

the death of false images, which is itself guidance

discipline that stabilizes a drifting system

consequences that teach what words cannot

practices that re-center attention when it scatters

truth surfacing through friction, not despite it
This is not the guidance I wanted. But it is the guidance I need.

What I Want Now
I do not want performative religion. I do not want borrowed certainty. I do not want to pretend trust I do not yet have.
What I want is more difficult: an honest relationship with what is real, without distortion.
If Allah is real, false images of Allah should die. If revelation is real, it should survive scrutiny. If prayer is real, it should transform something real in me. If guidance is real, it should help me become more truthful, stable, and alive.
That is the standard. Functional. Testable. Not sentimental.

For Others at the Edge
Maybe you are reading this from your own edge. Maybe your inherited image of God no longer works. Maybe religious language triggers pain. Maybe community culture feels shallow. Maybe certainty feels impossible. Maybe you are tired of being told to suppress honest questions.
Then hear this: the collapse of an image is not always the collapse of truth.
Sometimes what breaks is the container, not the signal. Sometimes grief is part of refinement. Sometimes doubt is not rebellion but refusal of falsehood. Sometimes faith does not disappear when the idol dies. Sometimes something more honest begins.

Where I Stand
I do not stand in triumphant certainty. I stand in humility. There is much I do not know. I cannot grasp the ground of being with a human mind. I cannot force trust on command.
But I can remain sincere. I can keep examining what is true. I can reject distortion. I can recite al-Fātiḥa again, not because I have answers, but because the map is accurate. Because the hunger is true. Because the path is narrow. And because I am still walking.
Wishing you all a beautiful week.

title: When the Mind Sees Its Own Model
text:
I have been trying to write from a place quieter than certainty.
Not from the part of me that wants to win an argument. Not from the part that wants to perform belief, defend a position, or turn pain into a philosophy. From somewhere slower than that. Somewhere more honest. The place in me that has been tired of pretending, but still unwilling to become cynical. The place that wants truth, even when truth does not arrive cleanly.
For a while now, much of what I have shared has moved through Islam. I know that may have surprised some people. Maybe it sounded like a sudden turn, or like I was reaching for a ready-made identity after too much uncertainty. But from the inside, it has not felt like that.
It has felt like being pulled into questions I could no longer keep abstract.
Prayer stopped being only an idea. Discipline stopped being only a concept. Surrender stopped being only a word people use when they have given up thinking. The question of God, the question of death, the question of what kind of life is actually worth living, these things entered my days. They entered my body. They entered the small choices where a person discovers what they really worship.
And yes, I know Islam carries weight. Cultural weight. Political weight. Historical weight. Emotional weight. I understand why people hesitate. I hesitate too. I carry questions, tensions, fears, resistance, and the need to be honest about all of it.
But underneath the language of any one tradition, there is a deeper thread I keep returning to:
What is a human being?
What is this “self” we spend our lives defending, explaining, narrating, protecting, improving, and sometimes suffering under?
And what becomes possible when the mind begins to see that the “me” it lives through is not a fixed object, but a living model, something constructed, maintained, corrected, wounded, trained, and oriented?
This essay is my attempt to return to that underlying thread with more tenderness than certainty. It is not a rejection of what I have been learning religiously. It is part of the same movement. Because if consciousness can see through some of its own constructions, the next question is not only, “How do I become free?” It is also: free for what? Oriented toward what? Guided by what?
We are finite. We will die. We do not access reality directly, not completely. We live through perception, memory, language, longing, fear, history, and hope. And we may never reach the kind of absolute certainty the anxious mind keeps demanding before it agrees to live.
That has been one of the hardest things for me to face.
But maybe the absence of final certainty does not mean the absence of truth. Maybe it means we have to become more careful, more humble, more sincere, and more responsible in how we orient ourselves.
A system that loses its illusions still needs direction. A mind that sees its own models still needs a way to live. And a human being who becomes aware of the self as process still has to ask what truth, goodness, responsibility, and God might mean. Or guidance.

Human beings do not live inside reality directly.
We live inside contact with reality, and then inside the meanings, predictions, memories, fears, hopes, and explanations the mind builds around that contact.
A sound becomes a threat. A silence becomes rejection. A desire becomes a plan. A wound becomes a worldview. A habit becomes a personality. A repeated story becomes “who I am.”
This is not a defect. It is how human life becomes possible.
Without models, there is no language, memory, science, morality, culture, religion, or long-term responsibility. We need maps to move through the world. We need identity to keep promises across time. We need stories to connect yesterday to tomorrow.
But the same capacity that makes human life coherent can also make it captive.
Because the mind does not only build models.
It forgets that it built them.
And then it bows before them as if they were reality itself.

The Mind Is Not a Window
The simplest mistake is to imagine perception as a window: the world is outside, the mind is inside, and experience is just reality passing cleanly through the glass.
But the mind is not a window. It is closer to a living prediction system.
At every moment, before conscious thought fully arrives, the nervous system is already asking practical questions:
What matters here?
What is safe?
What is dangerous?
What has happened before?
What should be ignored?
What action should be prepared?
What does this mean for me?
Perception is not invented from nothing. Reality presses in. The body responds to something real. But experience is always shaped by the organism meeting that reality: its memories, injuries, loyalties, training, language, expectations, and needs.
This is why two people can enter the same room and live through different worlds.
One notices threat. Another notices opportunity. One hears criticism. Another hears concern. One sees God’s order. Another sees randomness. One sees a stranger. Another sees a rival. Another sees someone to protect.
The room is real.
But the lived world is interpreted.

The Self Is Also a Model
The most intimate model is the one we rarely question: the self.
We often feel as if there is a solid inner owner at the center of experience, a fixed “me” who thinks thoughts, makes choices, has emotions, and remains the same underneath everything.
But if we look carefully, the self appears less like a single object and more like an ongoing coordination process.
It is assembled from many streams:
Bodily sensation
Memory
Emotion
Attention
Language
Habit
Moral commitments
Social roles
Wounds
Desires
Imagined futures
Stories repeated until they feel obvious
This does not mean the self is unreal. A pattern can be real without being a thing. A flame is real, though it is a process. A song is real, though it exists only through ordered movement. A life is real, though no single moment contains the whole of it.
The self is like that: real as a pattern, useful as an organizer, morally significant as the place where responsibility becomes personal.
But it is not as solid, private, or self-transparent as it feels.
This matters because much of our suffering comes not only from pain itself, but from fusion with the model around pain.
“I feel fear” becomes “I am weak.”
“I made a mistake” becomes “I am ruined.”
“I desire this” becomes “I need this.”
“I was hurt” becomes “the world is fundamentally unsafe.”
“I do not know” becomes “there is no truth.”
The mind turns experience into identity. Then identity starts defending itself.

The Story Often Arrives After the Reaction
One of the most humbling things to notice is that explanation often comes late.
A body tightens before a person says, “I just think you are wrong.”
A wound flares before a person says, “I am only being realistic.”
A craving moves before a person builds a philosophy around freedom.
A fear of uncertainty appears before a person declares certainty.
A need to belong forms before a person calls the group’s assumptions “truth.”
This does not mean reasons are fake. Human beings really can reason. We can discover truth, correct error, and discipline impulse.
But reason is not the only force speaking through us.
Sometimes what feels like clear judgment is a nervous system trying to protect itself. Sometimes what feels like moral clarity is anger wearing clean clothes. Sometimes what feels like independence is a refusal to be vulnerable. Sometimes what feels like “just who I am” is an old adaptation that has not yet been examined.
This is why honesty has to go deeper than having arguments.
A person can win an argument and still be hiding.
A person can explain themselves beautifully and still not be free.

The Beginning of Freedom
Freedom begins when the system starts to notice itself.
Not from above the body. Not outside life. Not in some pure detached state where nothing matters.
Freedom begins in the small, almost invisible gap where experience is no longer immediately obeyed.
You notice anger before the sentence leaves your mouth.
You notice shame before disappearing.
You notice the craving before turning it into a moral right.
You notice the tightening in the chest before calling it certainty.
You notice the identity-script before it takes the wheel.
You notice the worldview forming around a wound.
That moment is not enlightenment. It is not perfection. It may not even feel peaceful.
But it is a crack in automaticity.
And through that crack, responsibility enters.
Many traditions and disciplines have named this capacity in different ways: mindfulness, metacognition, decentering, cognitive defusion, muraqabah, watchfulness, remembrance, prayerful attention.
The vocabulary differs. The function is similar.
The mind becomes aware of part of its own modeling process.
It sees not only the thought, but the fact that it is thinking.
Not only the feeling, but the way the feeling is asking to become a story.
Not only the impulse, but the direction in which the impulse wants to carry the body.
And once something is seen, it is no longer absolute in the same way.

Awareness Is Not the Same as Truth
But here is where many modern conversations become too thin.
Seeing that the self is constructed does not automatically tell us how to live.
Awareness can create space, but space is not yet guidance.
A person can become less fused with identity and still become vain about being “beyond ego.”
A person can see through social conditioning and still drift into nihilism.
A person can understand their trauma and still excuse harm.
A person can become psychologically sophisticated and morally evasive.
A person can gain distance from old beliefs and then mistake emptiness for wisdom.
This is why the question cannot end at, “Is the self constructed?”
The deeper question is: constructed in relation to what?
If the mind is a modeling system, then the decisive issue is calibration.
What corrects the model?
What disciplines desire?
What interrupts self-deception?
What teaches the self that it is not sovereign?
What orients freedom toward goodness rather than mere possibility?
This is where moral seriousness becomes unavoidable.
A mind that sees its own models still needs a standard by which those models can be judged. A person who becomes less trapped in identity still has to choose whether to become more honest, more merciful, more responsible, more surrendered to truth, or simply more flexible in serving appetite.
Awareness opens the hand.
It does not decide what the hand should hold.

Why Religion Enters the Question
This is one reason religion has become difficult for me to treat as merely an identity category.
At its worst, religion can become another model the ego uses to harden itself: a badge, a tribe, a superiority system, a way to stop asking questions.
But at its best, religion is not a costume for the self. It is a discipline placed over the self.
It says: you are not the final measure.
Your moods are not revelation.
Your desires are not law.
Your intelligence is not innocence.
Your pain is real, but it is not automatically truth.
Your freedom matters, but it must be ordered toward what is good.
This is also why surrender is not necessarily the enemy of freedom. Sometimes surrender is what protects freedom from becoming worship of the self.
To pray, to restrain oneself, to repent, to tell the truth when it costs something, to return again and again to a reference beyond private appetite, these are not merely beliefs in the abstract. They are ways of retraining the model.
They are practices of calibration.
The question, of course, remains whether the reference is true. That question cannot be bypassed. A useful illusion is still an illusion. But neither can the human need for orientation be dismissed as weakness. The need for guidance may be one of the most honest facts about us.

When the Model Breaks Open
Sometimes this process becomes more intense.
Through prayer, contemplation, grief, trauma, meditation, crisis, love, or sustained attention, the ordinary self-model can loosen dramatically.
The story that once felt like a prison may suddenly look like a story.
The “I” that seemed so solid may appear more like movement.
The boundary between observer and observed may become less obvious.
The world may feel charged with meaning, stripped of meaning, or frighteningly open.
These states should be handled carefully.
They are not automatic proof of any worldview.
They can heal, but they can also destabilize.
They can humble the ego, but they can also create a more subtle ego that thinks it has transcended ordinary people.
Still, they reveal something important: the ordinary sense of self is not the only possible mode of consciousness.
Human beings can experience the constructed nature of their inner world more directly than they usually do.
And when that happens, the question becomes urgent:
If my familiar self is not the final ground, then what is?
What remains trustworthy when the old center loosens?
What can receive the weight the ego used to carry?

The Modern Danger: Captured Attention
This matters now because we live in an environment designed to colonize our models.
Every day, systems compete to shape what we notice, fear, desire, and believe.
Algorithms learn our sensitivities. Outrage becomes a rhythm. Identity becomes a market. Anxiety becomes engagement. Loneliness becomes a product. Moral language becomes a weapon. Attention becomes the battlefield on which the self is continuously rewritten.
A mind that cannot observe its own conditioning is easy to steer.
Not because it is stupid, but because it is human.
If I do not see how anger is being fed, I will call manipulation conviction.
If I do not see how fear is being trained, I will call panic realism.
If I do not see how desire is being intensified, I will call compulsion freedom.
If I do not see how identity is being rewarded, I will call performance authenticity.
The question of self-awareness is therefore not private self-help. It is civilizational.
A society of unobserved minds becomes easy to govern through impulse.
And intelligence alone will not save it.
Intelligence without humility becomes domination.
Awareness without conscience becomes manipulation.
Freedom without orientation becomes drift.
Power without truth becomes corruption.

A Small Practice of Reorientation
The work begins smaller than we want it to.
Not with a final theory of consciousness.
Not with perfect certainty.
Not with a transformed personality.
With one honest interruption.
Try this once today:

Notice a moment of tension, craving, defensiveness, resentment, shame, or fear.

Pause long enough to feel it in the body.

Name what is happening without turning it into identity: fear is here, anger is here, craving is here, a story is forming.

Ask: What am I being pulled to believe right now?

Ask: Is that belief true, or only familiar?

Ask: What response would make me more honest, more merciful, and more responsible?

Do the smallest next thing that answers that question.
This is not dramatic.
But it is real.
Each time the model is seen, it becomes a little less total.
Each time impulse is interrupted by truthfulness, the self becomes a little less enslaved.
Each time awareness joins conscience, freedom becomes more than a feeling. It becomes character.

Maybe maturity is not the destruction of the self.
Maybe it is the purification of our relationship to it.
To have a self, but not worship it.
To use thought, but not confuse thought with reality.
To carry identity, but not become imprisoned by it.
To build models, but remain humble enough to let truth correct them.
And perhaps, at the deepest level, to realize that the mind’s greatest power is not simply that it can construct a world.
It is that it can notice the construction.
It can pause before obedience.
It can ask what deserves trust.
It can become available to guidance.
A system that loses its illusions still needs direction.
A mind that sees its own models still needs truth.
And a human being who becomes aware of the self as process still has to ask what kind of life that awareness is meant to serve.
Subscribe now

title: When Guidance Rewrites the Self: A Revert’s Account of Collapse, Reorientation, and Learning to Trust the Unseen
text:
What you’re about to read is not a neat “conversion story.” It is the record of a mind that had already spent years dismantling naïve assumptions about identity, perception, and control, then encountered Islam not as a lifestyle package, but as an orienting reality that reached deeper than concepts. The result was not gentle. It was an internal reordering that brought grief, authority trauma, doubt, and clarity into the same room. If you recognize yourself in this, you are not alone.

The precondition: a decade of deconstruction before Islam
Long before I prayed in a mosque or said the shahāda, I was already deconstructing the idea of a sovereign self. I had spent more than ten years looking closely at how a person is formed and steered, by trauma, by language, by social conditioning, by nervous-system states, by history, and by the stories a mind tells itself in order to survive.
From that angle, “self” did not feel like a king. It felt like a coalition: processes negotiating for safety, meaning, belonging, continuity. Awareness itself did not feel neutral; it felt conditioned. Even perception, what I thought was “just seeing”, showed itself to be interpretive, biased, shaped by prior experience and by the body’s state.
And once you see that, a very serious question emerges, one that is more than philosophical and more than psychological. It becomes existential.
If I am not sovereign, if I cannot trust that my own internal model is clean, updated, and reliable, then what am I supposed to align with?
I could not honestly orient my life around “human things” as ultimate reference points. People drift. People react. People betray their own stated values. Cultures move in waves. Even my own inner states fluctuate. I could still love people, work with people, care for people. But I could not treat human consensus or human authority as a final compass.
So I was left with a hinge-question that was not theoretical:
Who updates the model?
If the human being is something like a cybernetic system, constantly predicting, constantly correcting, what is the trustworthy reference point for that correction? What regulates the regulator?

The encounter: not a club, not a costume, but an orienting force
When I began reading Qur’an and approaching prayer, something in me recognized an answer, not as a tidy proposition, but as a new kind of orientation.
It was less like, “I have adopted a new identity,” and more like, “I have encountered a reference point that does not drift.”
Islam arrived to me as an axis: a way of facing something beyond the social field, beyond my personal history, beyond my private preferences, beyond my trauma-conditioned reflexes. It did not feel like a human authority competing with other human authorities. It felt like a different category altogether.
And that is precisely why it did not come gently.
Because the moment you place the ego, your sense of “I decide, I verify, I control”, in front of something outside yourself, everything in you that has been trained to fear authority wakes up. If you carry authority trauma, your body does not hear “God” as pure transcendence. Your body hears a familiar warning: domination, coercion, unpredictability, betrayal. The nervous system translates “above me” into “danger.”
So my first experiences of prayer were not simply comfort. They were confrontation.
Not because prayer is violent, but because alignment exposes what is misaligned.

Authority trauma: why turning to God can feel like touching a wound
If you have learned, through real experience, that authority is unsafe, if you have been harmed under “authority,” controlled by “authority,” dismissed by “authority,” manipulated by “authority”, then the instruction “submit to God” can trigger a deep internal paradox.
On one side: the longing for a true north that cannot be corrupted.
On the other side: the body’s certainty that “trusting what is above me” is how I get hurt.
This creates a distinctive struggle that is often invisible from the outside. It is not merely intellectual doubt. It is not mere stubbornness. It is an embodied resistance that can contain terror, grief, rage, and profound vulnerability.
At first, I had to confront the fact that I didn’t merely distrust people; I started from not trusting God, because who is God to me, experientially? What am I relating to? How do you relate to something you cannot see, cannot place in a category, cannot verify the way you verify ordinary objects?
If I was honest, I could not begin from certainty. I could only begin from need.
And I kept showing up anyway.

The human noise: culture, hypocrisy, and the search for a clear signal
One of the hardest parts of being drawn toward Islam is that Islam is carried by humans, and humans are human.
I was not looking for human messiness. I already knew messiness; I lived it. I wasn’t trying to erase the world’s complexity or pretend people are pure. I was simply searching for a clear signal, something higher than reactivity, higher than social performance, higher than hypocrisy.
But what I encountered, alongside real beauty, was also noise:
marriage pressure, cultural expectations, drama, performativity, contradictions, petty dynamics, sometimes even a sense that people were more attached to identity than to truth.
This can be uniquely painful for someone who is sincerely seeking God rather than seeking belonging. Because when you are looking for a stable reference point, it hurts to find so much instability in the social layer that claims to represent it.
And yet, this becomes part of the spiritual education too: the difference between God and the human channel; the difference between the religion as an orienting truth and the community as a messy organism still learning to embody that truth.
It is possible to be drawn to the signal and exhausted by the noise at the same time.

The “purification” effect: why alignment brings grief and trauma to the surface
What happened next felt like a cleansing, but not in the simplistic sense people sometimes romanticize.
It was more like this: when you align with something you experience as higher, clearer, and less reactive, your internal system begins to reorganize around it. That reorganization makes certain inner material impossible to ignore.
Grief surfaced.
Authority trauma surfaced.
Old defense mechanisms surfaced.
Places where I was split, where I could not surrender, where I could not see clearly, became visible.
Not because I was going hunting for trauma, but because the orientation itself changed the contrast. Like turning on a bright light in a room you’ve always navigated in dimness: you see the dust, not because you created dust, but because you can finally see.
This is one reason the process can feel “not gentle.” You are not only learning new ideas; you are meeting the parts of you that were built to survive in a different operating environment. Those parts do not disappear just because the mind has found a new framework. They come forward to negotiate, to resist, to protect, to ask whether this new “authority” will hurt you too.

The collapse: when the demand for absolute certainty breaks
At some point, the process became so intense that I experienced an existential collapse. Reality itself seemed to demonstrate that my model of reality was not as trustworthy as I believed. I saw how much I think in absolutes, how much I demand certainty, and how dependent my mind is on the body: on sleep, on food, on rest, on regulation.
This is a humbling kind of knowledge. It is not glamorous. It is not mystical in the Instagram sense. It is the blunt realization that you are not a floating intellect; you are a finite organism. Your thoughts do not stand above biology. Your “certainty” is often downstream of your nervous system.
In my most distressed moments, I found myself demanding something impossible: absolute certainty from God, from reality, from everything.
And eventually I had to face the deeper question hiding inside that demand:
Why am I demanding an absolute that does not exist in the way I’m imagining it?
For me, that demand was connected to sovereignty. If I can obtain absolute certainty, then I can remain the verifier; I can remain the one in control. The demand for certainty can be the ego’s last attempt to keep the throne.
The collapse did not give me easy answers. It did something more important: it rearranged the posture of my seeking.

A crucial shift: from concept-possession to honest orientation
I still cannot say, in a simplistic way, “I believe in God,” if what that means is: I possess a complete concept, and I can state it with confidence, and my mind can contain the thing I’m relating to.
I cannot form a concept of God that does not borrow from the past, because every concept is built out of prior experience, and prior experience is limited, conditioned, human. Any concept I build will be a “past prior.” By definition it will not be the Reality itself, only an approximation I carry.
So I stopped trying to win God with conceptual capture.
Instead, something more grounded emerged: practice as orientation; prayer as posture.
I go to the mat.
I admit I am not sovereign.
I ask for guidance.
I refuse to pretend certainty.
I show up anyway.
This may look like weakness from the outside. From the inside, it felt like the first honest relationship I could form with the unknown: not through possession, but through humility; not through control, but through surrender; not through perfect comprehension, but through faithful returning.
In that sense, my “faith” did not begin as a claim. It began as an honest request.

What I learned about myself: finitude, dependence, and the end of the hero fantasy
One of the most sobering lessons was the collapse of the hero narrative, the story that I could carry myself by sheer force of intellect, will, or moral intensity.
The process showed me, repeatedly, that I am dependent:
dependent on rest,
dependent on nourishment,
dependent on regulation,
dependent on the mercy of circumstances I don’t control.
This is not a punishment. It is simply reality. But seeing it clearly can feel like grief, especially if you have survived by being hyper-independent, hyper-competent, or emotionally self-sufficient.
Islam did not merely ask me to “believe.” It asked my whole organism to accept its actual condition: created, contingent, needy.
And in that need, a different kind of strength can grow, one that doesn’t require pretending to be invulnerable.

What emerged after the turbulence: the quiet ethics of “do less harm”
Something surprising happened as I moved through the collapse and the reorientation.
I began to feel more aware that my presence matters.
Not in the grandiose sense of legacy or immortality projects. I am finite. I will die. But precisely because of that, I began to care more about the quality of my existence here: not to be “good” as a brand, and not to perfect myself as an ego trophy, but to live in a way that reduces harm.
This shift mattered to me because it felt like a clean output: simple, unromantic, real.
To do less harm.
To be more honest.
To be less reactive.
To be more responsible with what I bring into the world.
This did not arise as moral performance. It arose as the natural consequence of decentering the ego as the ultimate reference point. When you stop treating the self as the sun, you start seeing other lives as real in a different way.

The hidden difficulty of being a revert: no elders, no container, no language for what’s happening
There is a particular pain that comes with experiencing something this intense as a revert: you often have no one to talk to who can actually recognize it.
Many communities can teach you rules, basics, rituals, and surface-level identity. They may offer a course. They may offer a checklist. They may offer social belonging.
But if you are undergoing a deep internal reorganization, especially one tangled with trauma, epistemic honesty, nervous-system upheaval, and existential collapse, you may not find a container for it.
You may not find an elder who can say, calmly: “Yes. This happens. You are not crazy. This is a real stage. Here is how to walk through it without breaking yourself.”
Instead, you can feel alone with an experience that is too spiritual for purely clinical language, and too psychologically complex for simplistic religious language. And if you turn toward mental-health frameworks, you may fear being pathologized. If you turn toward religious spaces, you may fear being misunderstood or flattened into clichés.
That loneliness is not a small detail. It is one of the hardest parts.
Because the process itself is already demanding. Doing it without companionship can make it feel unbearable.

If you relate: you are not alone, and you are not failing
If any of this resonates, if you recognize the collision between authority trauma and surrender, if you know what it is to seek God while being unable to manufacture certainty, if you feel the ache of human hypocrisy while still sensing a higher signal, if you have experienced the surfacing of grief and trauma through prayer and alignment, then hear this clearly:
You are not alone.
And you are not failing because your path isn’t tidy.
There are people whose stories are quiet and smooth, and there are people whose stories are intense and jagged. Some people are carried into faith by belonging; some are dragged into honesty by collapse. Some feel immediate certainty; others are granted a slower, more austere gift: sincerity without pretense.
If you can only begin with, “I am not sovereign; I need guidance,” that is still a beginning.
If all you can do is return to the mat without certainty, that is still returning.
If you feel shattered by the process, that does not mean it is false; it may mean it is real and you are actually inside it.
The work is not to force yourself into a polished narrative. The work is to keep walking in honesty—without romanticizing suffering, without denying the nervous system, without confusing human culture for God, without demanding an absolute that your mind cannot hold.
What this experience has become for me, in the simplest terms
Years of deconstruction left me unable to worship the self or trust human drift as ultimate reference.
Islam did not give me a human authority to replace other human authorities.
It introduced an orienting possibility beyond my old model.
That orientation triggered reorganization:
authority trauma surfaced,
grief surfaced,
defenses surfaced,
certainty collapsed,
the body’s limits became undeniable,
and a different posture emerged, humble, honest, present.
I still cannot claim to contain God as a concept.
But I can return.
I can ask.
I can align.
And I can try to live in a way that does less harm.
If you are in the middle of your own collapse and rebuilding, may this be a small companionship: proof that the experience exists, that it can be named without being reduced, and that you do not have to carry it entirely alone.

title: The Great Decoupling: Beyond the Buffer and Back to the Source
text:
Photo by Timothy Chan on Unsplash
Why the Modern Ego Mistakes the Map for the Territory
After months of wading through messages attempting to manipulate me into marriage, I saw the void with a terrifying clarity. These overtures weren’t driven by a shared devotion to the Divine, but by social utility, a desire for the “domesticated” security of a system. Not a single person carried the spark I was searching for.
I realized then that my quest wasn’t for “Islam” as a codified social institution, but for that which Islam has been pointing toward all along. I was looking for the Source that made the Prophet (PBUH) possible; the same Light that guided those before him: The Hanifs.
The Hanifs were the primordial seekers. They didn’t follow a managed “crop” of revelation; they received direct feedback from the very grounds of existence.

The Industrial Agriculture of the Soul
My research into agriculture, surplus, and the rise of complex civilizations finally clicked into place. We often speak of the “Garden” as a mythic past, but it represents a cybernetic reality: a time when humans were tuned into direct feedback loops with Allah.
When we transitioned from nomadic life to sedentary complexity, we experienced The Great Decoupling. With agriculture came:
Surplus and Hoarding: The birth of systemic inequality.
The Buffer: We created models, maps, and abstractions to manage our complexity.
The Domesticated Self: We traded the unpredictable “wild” feedback of a personal spiritual journey for the safety and predictability of a social container.
If agriculture is the domestication of the land, organized religion is often the domestication of revelation. We replaced the diverse, “wild” forest of direct experience with a monocrop of dogma. We stopped looking at the Spring and started worshipping the Faucet.

Civilizational Overshoot and the Ego
As a student of cybernetics and control theory, I see why civilizations collapse: they mistake the buffer for reality.
In a nomadic state, if the ego gets too big, the feedback is immediate and often fatal. Nature is a master at lowering the ego. But in our modern “domesticated” world, the consequences of our actions are delayed by systems, wealth, and technology. This creates the illusion of Istighna, the Quranic concept of “perceived self-sufficiency.”
Because our feedback is delayed, the ego begins to believe it is the center of the universe. We live in a hall of mirrors, identifying with our status and our labels, forgetting that we are creatures entirely dependent on the Ground of Being.

Maintenance for the “Wild” Soul
I have deconstructed everything. Every day, I ask Allah to strip away the false and reveal the Real (Al-Haqq). I am searching for that intact Fitra, the original disposition that remembers the covenant we made before we were even born.
I now see prayer not as a social ritual, but as maintenance. It is a deliberate attempt to short-circuit the delay of the modern world and invite direct feedback back in. When we put our heads to the ground in Sujood, we are manually lowering the ego’s shields. We are putting our intellect, the seat of our “buffered” pride, on the soil, reconnecting with the Source.

The Path of the Stranger
We have reached the era the Prophet (PBUH) forewarned:

“Islam began as something strange, and it will return to being strange just as it began, so glad tidings to the strangers.”
He spoke of a time when the community would be numerous but hollow, like foam upon the ocean, decoupled from the substance of truth.
To be a Hanif today is to be a stranger. It is to bypass the artificial fences of men and seek the direct guidance that guided Adam, Ibrahim, and Muhammad (PBUH) before the world became a grid. It is to remember what my grandmother tried to preserve in me when she taught me to sing with the wind and touch the trees: that we are not separate from the Real.
The rules are just guardrails. The map is not the territory. I am stepping off the path of the domesticated and returning to the wild, direct, and terrifyingly beautiful feedback of the Divine.

Are you living in the buffer, or are you ready to face the Source?
