title: Learning to Live as Someone Who Is Not Sovereign
text:
I did not come to Islam because I wanted an identity. I came because I reached the limit of self-reference.
For years I contemplated the self, consciousness, trauma, power, perception, nervous system regulation, and the structures that shape human behavior. I learned that the ego is not something you simply “remove.” It always returns, because it has a function. It helps this particular life survive, choose, protect boundaries, interpret signals, and move through the world.
The problem is not that the self exists.
The problem is when the self mistakes itself for the altar.
That is where everything begins to distort.
The self is not self-sustaining. It is not sovereign. It is not the final authority. It is dependent, contingent, limited, and constantly shaped by forces it did not create. Body, memory, language, trauma, culture, biology, desire, fear, attention, history, all of these move through us before we even claim “I.”
And yet modern life teaches us to live as if the self is ultimate.
Define yourself. Express yourself. Brand yourself. Optimize yourself. Trust yourself. Become yourself. Perform yourself. Protect yourself. Display yourself.
At first this can feel like freedom. But eventually it becomes exhausting, because the self was never designed to carry the weight of ultimate meaning.
This is where Islam began to make sense to me.
Not as organized religion. Not as communal performance. Not as inherited identity. Not as men claiming ownership over God. But as orientation.
Islam, at its deepest, confronted the exact illusion I had been circling for years: the illusion of self-sovereignty.
The Qur’an does not present the human being as the center of reality. It presents the human being as dependent, forgetful, morally responsible, and in need of guidance. It also repeatedly warns about arrogance, heedlessness, hypocrisy, corruption, and people using religion itself for power. That matters. The tradition is not naive about human distortion.
This is why I became interested in the inner dimension of Islam, especially Sufism, tazkiyah, dhikr, and the purification of intention. Historically, Sufism developed as a major current of Islamic spirituality focused on remembrance of Allah, purification of the heart, and ihsan: worshipping Allah as though you see Him, and if you do not see Him, knowing that He sees you. Early ascetic figures such as Hasan al-Basri were remembered for piety and criticism of political excess under the Umayyads, which fits the pattern I keep noticing: sincere spirituality often emerges as a response to religious power becoming worldly.
That does not mean every Sufi group is pure. Humans corrupt everything. But the impulse toward sincerity, humility, remembrance, and ego-purification is real.
The Book of Assistance by Imam Abdallah ibn Alawi al-Haddad has been important for me because it does not treat religion as shallow identity. It keeps returning to intention, vigilance, certainty, humility, and sincerity. It does not say: destroy the self. It says, in effect: stop mistaking yourself for the center.
That distinction matters.
There is a kind of contemplation that still remains self-referential. You can observe yourself endlessly, analyze your ego endlessly, map every trauma response, every desire, every distortion, and still remain trapped inside the self. The observer becomes another ego position. The self becomes its own sacred object.
Eventually the question becomes:
What is all this contemplation oriented toward?
If contemplation only returns me to myself, then it is still circular.
This is what I am realizing now. The issue is not simply understanding the ego. The issue is being released from the ego’s illusion of sovereignty.
That is where submission begins to make sense.
Not submission to human domination. Not submission to corrupt communities. Not submission to men, institutions, politics, or religious performance.
Submission to Allah.
To Al-Haqq, the Real.
And even here, I have to be honest: I do not possess perfect certainty. I do not have total intellectual control over metaphysics, history, hadith transmission, or every ritual practice. I know enough about power to know that human religious systems accumulate distortion. Hadith transmission itself was historically contested, and hadith sciences developed partly because fabrication and weak transmission were real problems.
But uncertainty does not erase orientation.
That is what I am learning from the chapter on certainty.
Certainty is not omniscience. It is not “I solved reality.” It is not ideological rigidity. It is not emotional comfort. In The Book of Assistance, certainty grows through listening deeply, observing reality, and living according to the truth one already recognizes. It is strengthened through revelation, attention to the signs in the horizons and within the self, and sincere striving.
That changes the question.
Instead of asking, “Can I possess total certainty before I move?” the question becomes:
Can I live truthfully from what I already recognize?
I recognize that I am not sovereign.
I recognize that the ego distorts.
I recognize that modern self-worship is unstable.
I recognize that power corrupts human systems.
I recognize that I cannot be my own ultimate ground.
I recognize that I need orientation beyond myself.
That is not full possession of Truth. But it is enough to begin walking.
This also changed my relationship to hijab. I used to experience it mainly through the frame of gender control, community identity, and male regulation. Those distortions are real and cannot be ignored. But I also began to see another layer: hijab as embodied remembrance. Not self-erasure, but de-centering the ego. A reminder that I am not here merely to perform myself, display myself, or organize my life around the gaze.
That does not mean the cloth is magic. A person can wear hijab and worship the ego. A person can refuse hijab and still be sincerely seeking Allah. But I began to understand the deeper possibility: modesty as attention regulation, containment, humility, and orientation.
Not losing the self.
Putting the self in its proper place.
This is the core of what I am learning.
The self remains responsible. It still acts, chooses, serves, studies, works, prays, and cares. I work in nursing. I serve people in their vulnerability. That matters. Islam cannot become abstract spirituality detached from action. Intention has to become embodied.
But the self is not ultimate.
That is the difference.
The Qur’an says there is no compulsion in religion; truth and error are made distinct, and the human being must choose orientation. For me, that choice is not simple social belonging. It is not naive trust in Muslims. It is not pretending corruption does not exist.
It is choosing to keep turning toward Allah despite human distortion.
And that is where I am right now.
Not finished. Not certain in the shallow sense. Not absorbed into community. Not free from ego. Not beyond doubt.
But learning to live as someone who is not sovereign.
Learning that contemplation must eventually become surrender.
Learning that the ego cannot be killed, but it can be seen.
Learning that the self can remain alive, responsible, intelligent, and dignified without being enthroned.
Learning that truth is not something I possess.
It is something I must keep orienting toward.
And maybe that is where real faith begins.

title: The Illusion of Autonomy: Sovereignty, Freedom, and the Real Chain of Worship
subtitle: Most “freedom” is just a new master. Liberation begins when worship is named, and returned to Allah.
text:
Photo by Bob Jenkin on Unsplash
Why this matters
Modern language is soaked in autonomy: self-made, self-defined, my truth, my body, my choice. It sounds empowering, almost sacred. But it smuggles in a hidden assumption: that the self is a neutral throne capable of ruling itself. It isn’t. Something always sits on that throne: Allah, the nafs, the crowd, money, fear, desire, image.
The modern self does not stop worshipping when it abandons religion. Worship doesn’t vanish; it relocates. This is the illusion of autonomy: that freedom means having no master. It doesn’t. It means forgetting who your master already is—and the cost of that forgetting is enormous. What we call “freedom” often becomes appetite without orientation, identity without grounding, and choice without truth.
Islam offers a brutal correction: you are not sovereign. And that is not oppression. It is the beginning of liberation.

The confusion of terms
A lot of modern confusion is just sloppy definition. We use freedom, autonomy, and sovereignty as if they were interchangeable. They aren’t, and each word carries a different metaphysical claim.
Autonomy says: I am the author of my own law. The self as legislator, my values, my truth, my meaning. It sounds noble until you ask what this “self” really is. Is it stable? Rational? unified? Pure? Anyone who has watched their own mind for five minutes knows the answer.
Sovereignty says: There is no higher authority above me. Historically, sovereignty belonged to kings and states. Modernity internalized it, then democratized it: now the individual self becomes the final court of appeal. Nothing above it. Nothing judging it. Nothing correcting it. That isn’t liberation. It is theological displacement.
Freedom, in modern speech, often collapses into infinite permission. But permission from whom, and permission for what? To consume? To indulge? To reinvent the self endlessly? A crucial distinction is usually ignored: there is freedom from constraint and there is freedom for truth. Modernity is obsessed with the first. Islam is concerned with the second. That difference changes everything.

You always serve something
One of the most uncomfortable truths modernity tries to avoid is simple: worship never disappears; it relocates. If Allah is not worshipped, something else will be. The nafs. Desire. Status. Romance. Money. The state. The market. The body. The crowd.
Every human being organizes life around an ultimate concern, something that quietly governs attention, sacrifice, fear, loyalty, time, and identity. That is worship, whether the word is used or not. The Qur’an warns that false gods are not only idols of stone; they are anything granted ultimate authority.
A ruthless diagnostic helps: what can you not disobey? What do you obey even when you know it harms you? Your phone? Your lust? Your need for validation? Your image? Your career script? Your ideology? Whatever it is, worship is already visible.

The self is fractured, so “self-rule” is unstable
The fantasy of autonomy depends on a myth: that the self is one coherent thing, an unbroken chooser with a stable center. But introspection (and psychology) says otherwise. Inside a person are competing systems: impulses, cravings, fears, trauma loops, social conditioning, status drives, attachment wounds, dopamine patterns.
So when someone says, “I chose this,” what often happened is less heroic: one subsystem won. The louder impulse won. The stronger fear won. The deeper conditioning won, and then the mind narrated it as “choice.” That is devastating for the modern story, because it means the self is frequently not acting freely. It is reacting, and then rationalizing.

Freedom without truth becomes slavery
Once freedom is defined as “I can do whatever I want,” the strongest internal and external forces take over. And what are those forces in late modern life? Dopamine engineering. Sexual stimulation on demand. Consumer manipulation. Algorithmic reinforcement. Status competition. Escapism packaged as self-care.
This is not freedom; it is behavioral capture.
Porn whispers, “You’re free,” while many cannot stop. Social media says, “Express yourself,” while most are quietly shaped by trends they did not choose. Consumer culture promises uniqueness by selling the same identity to everyone in different fonts. The self becomes programmable while believing it is sovereign. That is one of the cruelest illusions of modernity.

The hidden religion of modernity
Modernity claims neutrality. It isn’t neutral. It has sacred objects and unquestionable values; it simply refuses to call them sacred.
It reveres the sovereign self. It worships the nation-state and the market. It canonizes the body, authenticity, personal desire, and therapeutic comfort. Its moral language is everywhere: “live your truth,” “protect your peace,” “follow your heart,” “be unapologetically you.” These aren’t neutral suggestions; they function like commandments. Modernity did not abolish religion. It privatized God and deified preference.

Islam’s correction: freedom begins in ʿubūdiyyah
Islam begins where modernity resists: ʿubūdiyyah, servitude. To modern ears, servitude sounds like humiliation, because modernity hears only slavery. But Islam means something else entirely: alignment with Reality.
Allah is the only Master who does not exploit, manipulate, consume, or abandon. Everything else extracts. The nafs always demands more and gives less. Desire expands. The market never says “enough.” People’s approval is unstable and conditional. Only Allah is stable.
That is why tawḥīd is not merely theology. It is existential reordering.

The paradox of submission
Here is the paradox: when you submit to Allah, you become harder to enslave, by people, trends, lust, fear, loss, and even your own compulsions. Submission creates internal sovereignty, not because you have no master, but because you finally know your Master. And once worship is ordered correctly, you can say “no” with real strength: no to appetite, no to vanity, no to degradation, no to endless consumption.
Modern freedom rarely produces that kind of “no.”

Discipline is the price of freedom
A self that says “I’m free” but cannot fast, lower the gaze, control speech, leave the phone, keep promises, or pray on time is not free. It is managed, by cravings, by habits, by social pressure, by whatever pushes hardest.
Discipline is not the enemy of freedom; it is the condition for it. That is why Islam disciplines the body and the day: prayer, fasting, charity, modesty, dhikr, restraint. Not to shrink the self, but to free it.

If the word submission makes you uncomfortable, pause and ask honestly: what currently owns me? What owns my attention, my shame, my time? What do I obey even when I hate it?
That is where worship already lives.
The question is not whether you worship. The question is whether your worship is ordered rightly. And if it is not, the path back begins with naming the chain, and returning it to Allah. Because the deepest truth remains: you were never sovereign. And pretending to be has cost us more than we realize.

title: What the Peter Thiel Dialog Leak Actually Reveals: Civilizational Stress, Elite Positioning, and the Limits of Human Prediction
text:
my caption
There’s something almost ritualistic about the public reaction whenever elite networks are exposed.
People split into two camps immediately:
One says:
See? They control everything.
The other says:
It’s nothing. Just rich people networking.
Both responses miss the deeper structure.
The recent leak of Peter Thiel’s private network, Dialog, is not important because it proves omnipotent elite control.
It is important because it reveals what high-agency actors are paying attention to.
And what they are paying attention to tells us something about the shape of the world.
Not because they are prophets.
But because power tends to track instability early.

What is Dialog?
Dialog is a private invite-only network founded by Peter Thiel and Auren Hoffman.
Think less “secret society” and more strategic elite ecosystem.
Tech founders, investors, military strategists, political thinkers, academics, and media figures gather privately to discuss the future.
The leak exposed:

internal rankings of members by influence

matchmaking systems

retreat topics

future scenario planning

private communications
That matters.
Not because it is sinister by default.
But because all civilizations generate high-bandwidth private coordination systems.
This is not new.
Sumer had priesthoods. Rome had senatorial circles. Empires always generate inner chambers.
The form changes. The pattern remains.

What are they worried about?
This is where it becomes interesting.
Again and again, the same stressors appear:
1. Demographic collapse
Birth rates across Europe, Japan, South Korea, and China are below replacement.
This changes everything.
No civilization survives long-term without generational continuity.
No births means:

pension instability

labor shortages

migration dependence

rising elderly populations

political contraction
This alone destabilizes the modern growth model.
2. Energy limits
Every civilization is fundamentally an energy conversion system.
We forget this because abstraction hides it.
But food, transport, computation, medicine, and infrastructure all rest on energetic throughput.
Elite circles know this.
They know complexity has metabolic costs.
And complexity without sufficient energy begins to cannibalize itself.
Joseph Tainter mapped this decades ago.
3. AI and labor
AI is not merely technological innovation.
It is social reorganization.
When cognitive labor becomes automatable:

expertise destabilizes

employment structures shift

legitimacy systems collapse

knowledge hierarchies flatten
And when human beings lose economic function, political volatility rises.
This is predictable.
4. Institutional trust collapse
Trust in media, governments, academia, medicine, and religion is degrading.
This creates what cybernetics would call signal distortion.
When signal integrity collapses:
coordination collapses.
Societies become harder to regulate.
Harder to govern.
Harder to trust.
5. Migration pressure
Climate stress. War. Economic asymmetry. Resource collapse.
All produce migration.
And receiving societies under pressure tend to contract.
This is not moral theory.
It is recurring human behavior.
Stress increases tribal sorting.
Always.

What does this tell us?
Not that “they know everything.”
But that the old stability model is weakening.
And elite actors are not solving this.
They are positioning.
That distinction matters.
Power rarely solves first.
It hedges first.
It diversifies first.
It prepares first.
That is what we are seeing.

But here is the real danger
The danger is not merely elite coordination.
The danger is what happens to ordinary perception when exposed to this.
People tend to do one of two things:
Worship power
Fear power
Both are errors.
Both distort.
Because power is contingent.
Always.
It rises. Coordinates. Protects itself. Then dissolves.
Every empire believed itself permanent.
None were.

The Islamic correction
This is where the Qur’an introduces something radically stabilizing.
Humans can model trends.
They can track probabilities.
They can anticipate constraints.
But they do not know the unseen.
That boundary is absolute.
And in times of civilizational stress, that distinction matters more than ever.
Because if your orientation becomes fear, you collapse before the system does.
The real task is not prediction.
It is orientation.
To stay soft without becoming naive. To stay perceptive without becoming paranoid. To stay informed without becoming possessed.
And most importantly:
to remember that all systems are contingent.
Only Allah is not.

The elites may know what is breaking.
But they do not know what comes after.
That belongs to Allah alone.

title: Hadith Jibril
text:
Photo by Tim de Groot on Unsplash
Tonight I am going to the Mosque, and the second course discussing the Hadith Jibril. I would like to share it with you. 
In Madinah, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was sitting with his companions when a stranger appeared, so strikingly clean and composed that everyone noticed. His clothes were intensely white, his hair intensely black, and yet no one recognized him as a traveler. He walked straight through the gathering and sat close to the Prophet ﷺ with an unusual familiarity: knees to knees, placing his hands on the Prophet’s thighs, as if a student before a teacher.
Then he began to ask, calmly and directly, questions that sounded simple, but were like keys turning in locks.
“O Muhammad, tell me about Islam.”
The Prophet ﷺ answered with the outward pillars: that you testify there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is His Messenger; establish prayer; give zakāh; fast Ramadan; and perform ḥajj if able.
The stranger replied: “You have spoken the truth.”
The companions were stunned: who asks a question and then confirms the answer like they already know?
“Then tell me about Īmān (faith).”
The Prophet ﷺ described the inward foundations: belief in Allah, His angels, His books, His messengers, the Last Day, and destiny, its good and its bitter.
Again the stranger said: “You have spoken the truth.”
“Then tell me about Iḥsān.”
Now it went deeper than actions and beliefs, into presence. The Prophet ﷺ said:
To worship Allah as though you see Him, and if you do not see Him, then know that He sees you.
“Then tell me about the Hour.”
The Prophet ﷺ answered with humility: the one being asked knows no more than the questioner.
So the stranger shifted:
“Then tell me about its signs.”
The Prophet ﷺ mentioned signs that disturb the usual order of the world: that a slave woman will give birth to her mistress, and that you will see barefoot, destitute shepherds competing with one another in building tall structures.
When the questions ended, the stranger rose and left as suddenly as he came. The companions sat in a kind of silence, the moment lingering. After a while, the Prophet ﷺ asked ʿUmar (ra):
“Do you know who the questioner was?”
He said: Allah and His Messenger know best.
The Prophet ﷺ said: “That was Jibrīl (Gabriel). He came to teach you your religion.”
And that is the heart of the story: the religion was laid out in a living scene, 
Islam: what you do
Īmān: what you hold as true
Iḥsān: how awake your heart is before Allah
taught not as abstract theory, but as an encounter that the companions never forgot.

title: The Collapse of Containment
subtitle: Why Modern Society Feels Like a Machine and Islam Feels Like Return
text:
Photo by Wylly Suhendra on Unsplash
I have been thinking a lot lately about what a society is actually for.
Not politically.
Not economically.
Structurally.
What is its function?
Because the more I study Islam, especially through the Hadith Jibril, the pillars, prayer, fasting, zakat, ihsan, the more I realize that religion, at least in its deepest form, is not just belief.
It is architecture.
It is a regulatory system.
And once you see that, modern society starts to look very strange.

Human beings did not evolve in hyper-complex systems
For most of human history, we lived in tribes.
Small.
Dense.
Relational.
A tribe solved fundamental human problems:
survival,
belonging,
protection,
knowledge transmission,
shared grief,
shared child-rearing,
ritual,
meaning.
Everything was immediate.
If someone suffered, it was visible.
If someone drifted, it was noticed.
If someone died, grief was collective.
The nervous system evolved in this.
Not in cities of millions.
Not in algorithmic feeds.
Not in endless anonymous exchanges.
That matters.
Because the nervous system still expects containment.

Modern society optimized for scale, not for souls
Modern civilization is brilliant.
It can coordinate millions of strangers.
It can move food across continents.
Power across grids.
Information across the globe.
But it does this through abstraction.
And abstraction has consequences.
Where tribes distributed human functions across relationships, modernity distributes them across institutions.
School teaches.
Therapists regulate.
Employers extract labor.
Hospitals care.
Apps mediate relationships.
Governments manage risk.
The person becomes fragmented.
The whole human gets split across systems.
And everyone is told:
regulate yourself.
Heal yourself.
Earn your place.
Build your life.
Find your meaning.
This is a massive burden.
It creates the fantasy of sovereignty.
That you must carry yourself.
That you must be your own tribe.
But human beings are not built for that.

This is why everyone feels alone
Modernity has given us access without containment.
You can talk to a thousand people and belong nowhere.
You can be visible and unseen.
Connected and unheld.
And I think this is why so many people feel psychologically unstable, spiritually fragmented, and relationally exhausted.
Not because they are weak.
But because the structure itself is dysregulating.
The environment is invasive.
Attention is constantly extracted.
Desire is constantly stimulated.
Comparison is constant.
Noise is endless.
The nafs is never left alone.

Islam suddenly makes sense as anti-drift architecture
This is what struck me.
Islam does not simply “tell you what to believe.”
It interrupts drift.
Prayer five times a day is repeated re-orientation.
It forces dependency.
It breaks ego continuity.
It says:
Stop.
Return.
Fasting weakens impulse.
Zakat weakens ownership.
Shahada reorders metaphysics.
Ihsan purifies intention.
Dhikr cleans perception.
What I am starting to understand is that Islam functions like a corrective cybernetic system.
Feedback.
Correction.
Return.
Without these repeated constraints, the human being drifts.
And so does society.

Maybe the problem isn’t that humans became worse
Maybe the problem is scale.
Too much scale.
Too much abstraction.
Too much individual burden.
Too little embodied containment.
And this changes how I think about community.
Lately, I went to the mosque.
I asked about marriage.
But what happened surprised me.
I did not leave with answers about men.
I left with sisters.
Deep, intelligent women who felt grounded.
And for the first time, I felt seen.
That mattered.
Because maybe Allah was showing me something.
Maybe before answers, He gives ecology.
Before structure, He gives community.
Before marriage, He gives witnesses.
That sequence matters.
Because no single person should carry the weight of your entire regulation.
Not a husband.
Not a teacher.
Not even yourself.
The tribe was never one person.
It was an ecology.

We do not need to return to the past
This is important.
I am not romanticizing tribes.
Tribal life was brutal.
But there was something structurally intact there that modernity has fractured.
The task now is not to “go backwards.”
It is to rebuild intentional micro-ecologies inside mass society.
Small circles.
Trusted people.
Shared worship.
Embodied repetition.
Slow trust.
Distributed care.
Maybe that is what community is.
Not an institution.
Not a brand.
But human beings orienting together toward what is higher than themselves.
And perhaps that is why Islam begins where it does:
Not with society.
Not with politics.
But with surrender.
Because without the right center, every system drifts.
Even the self.

title: The Idol of Certainty
text:
Today I sat in the mosque, reading The Hikam of Ibn ‘Ata’illah.
Not casually.
Desperately.
Because I am being confronted with something I do not want to accept:
I am not in control of outcomes.
Not marriage.
Not love.
Not children.
Not healing.
Not safety.
Only Allah is.
And this sounds obvious until reality forces you to actually live it.
That is when tawakkul stops being a beautiful concept and becomes a dismantling.
A death.
A stripping.
I met sisters today. We talked about marriage, trust, loss, and what it means to walk toward Allah while the future remains radically uncertain.
One of them told me:
“This is tawakkul.”
And I knew she was right.
Because the theme right now is not “finding a husband.”
It is not “getting what I want.”
It is not even “healing.”
It is trust.
Raw trust.
Not trust that things will go my way.
Not trust that if I do enough inner work, Allah will reward me with the exact life I desire.
But trust that whatever He gives, or withholds, is still provision.
That is much harder.
Because my nervous system still wants contracts.
If I obey, I want guarantees.
If I heal, I want safety.
If I open my heart, I want certainty.
But Allah owes me none of that.
And this is where Ibn ‘Ata’illah cut straight through me:
Competing endeavors will not breach the ramparts of destinies.
You can strive.
You can prepare.
You can pray.
You can long.
But striving cannot force decree.
This cuts directly against modern conditioning.
We are taught:
Work hard and you’ll get what you want.
But reality says:
Work hard and you’ll get what Allah has written.
Those are not the same.
And that difference matters.
Especially when it comes to love.
Because I carry bad data.
I have lost pregnancies.
I have been betrayed.
I have seen what masculine failure can do.
I have watched trust collapse.
So part of me wants to predict aggressively.
To decide now:
“There is no man for me.”
Because certainty, even painful certainty, feels safer than openness.
But that too is a form of control.
A premature closure against Allah’s unfolding.
And that is what I am learning:
Tawakkul is not passivity.
It is disciplined openness.
To act without possessing the outcome.
To use means without worshipping them.
To want without demanding.
To grieve without collapsing.
To remain open without fantasy.
To move toward Allah without turning Him into a machine that dispenses desires.
This may be one of the hardest lessons of my life.
Because I am discovering that my deepest idol was never romance.
It was certainty.
And Allah is dismantling it.
Not to punish me.
But to free me.

title: The Unseen Order: How Systems Theory Brought Me to Religion
text:
Photo by National Cancer Institute on Unsplash
I did not arrive at religion through inheritance. Not through culture. Not through family. Not through comfort. I arrived at it through systems.
For more than a decade I studied collapse, cybernetics, ecology, cognition, trauma, embodiment, and human behavior. I was trying to understand one question:
What governs us?
Not politically.
Not psychologically.
But fundamentally.
What structures perception?
What regulates action?
What shapes civilizations?
What shapes the self?
The deeper I went, the more one thing became impossible to ignore:
nothing is sovereign.
Not organisms.
Not minds.
Not empires.
Everything exists inside dependency.

The Death of the Sovereign Self
Cybernetics was one of the first cracks.
Norbert Wiener showed that living systems regulate through feedback. Action is never isolated. Every movement enters a loop of consequence, correction, adaptation.
The organism is not autonomous.
It is relational.
Then came biology.
Lynn Margulis shattered the myth of isolated individuality. Life itself emerged through symbiosis. What we call “self” is layered cooperation.
Your body is not singular.
It is a negotiated ecology.
Then phenomenology.
Varela, Thompson, Rosch.
Perception is not passive reception of reality. It is enacted. Structured. Embodied. Conditioned by organism and history.
What I call “my world” is not simply “out there.”
It is co-emergent.
Already this destabilizes modern individualism.
But it gets worse.

Civilization Is Also a Nervous System
Studying collapse made the pattern unavoidable.
Tainter. Meadows. Smil.
Civilizations do not collapse because they “fail morally” in some simplistic sense.
They collapse because complexity exceeds energetic viability.
Every social order rests on flows:
energy
information
trust
food
coordination
meaning
When these destabilize, the whole structure trembles.
And suddenly I realized:
This is true psychologically too.
The self is also a complexity system.
Attention is energy allocation.
Desire is directional force.
Habit is recursive patterning.
Trauma is feedback distortion.
You are not “free” in the way modernity imagines.
You are conditioned.
Dependent.
Vulnerable to corruption.

The Religious Question Emerges
At first this did not make me religious.
It made me honest.
And honesty produced a terrifying insight:
If I am not sovereign, then what am I orienting toward?
Because orientation is inevitable.
If you do not consciously orient yourself, something else will.
Algorithms.
Desire.
Fear.
Status.
Sex.
Trauma.
Markets.
Empire.
Propaganda.
The nervous system always serves something.
Always.
That was the real beginning of religion for me.
Not belief.
Orientation.

William James and the Problem of Religious Experience
Recently I began reading The Varieties of Religious Experience by William James.
He makes a distinction that I think is essential.
Religious experience is real.
But its interpretation is not automatically true.
That is devastatingly important.
Because modern spirituality often collapses these together.
“I felt it, therefore it is true.”
No.
James shows that human beings are profoundly shaped by unseen objects.
Memory can govern behavior.
Future possibility can alter the body.
An idea can reorganize an entire life.
The unseen is not weak because it is unseen.
In fact, it often has more power than visible reality.
This struck me deeply.
Because this is exactly what religion fundamentally is:
alignment with the unseen.
But James warns:
Intensity is not proof.
Ecstasy is not proof.
Visions are not proof.
Certainty is not proof.
This matters.
Because trauma has intensity too.
Fantasy has intensity too.
Desire has intensity too.
Psychosis has intensity too.
So what separates revelation from projection?
That became the central question.

Islam Entered Here
When I read the Qur’an, something happened.
Not emotionally.
Structurally.
It described reality the way reality had already begun appearing to me.
Dependence.
Accountability.
Limits.
Feedback.
Consequences.
Veils.
Corruption.
Forgetfulness.
Remembrance.
Submission.
Not as domination.
As alignment.
Islam did not feel like ideological conversion.
It felt like ontological recognition.
The Qur’an’s insistence on ghayb.. the unseen, suddenly made sense.
Not as irrationality.
But as the deeper layer beneath visible process.
The visible world is not self-explanatory.
It rests on an order beyond itself.
And human beings are constantly being calibrated by what they worship.

The Real Danger
But there is danger here.
Religion can become ego.
Identity.
Performance.
Certainty addiction.
Institutional capture.
Male hierarchy.
Fantasy.
Group psychology.
Projection.
William James helped sharpen this for me:
Religious experience must be tested.
Not by how intense it feels.
But by what it produces.
Does it make you more truthful?
More sober?
More accountable?
More humble?
More merciful?
More capable of reality?
This is where I think many people get lost.
They worship the feeling instead of the truth.

What I Am Learning
I am learning that surrender is not passivity.
It is accurate placement.
To know your scale.
To know your dependency.
To know that you are not the source.
Not of your breath.
Not of your thoughts.
Not of your life.
Religion, at its best, is not escape from reality.
It is deeper contact with it.
And maybe that is why after all the books, all the systems, all the collapse models, all the psychology, all the analysis…
I ended up here.
At the oldest human question.
What is the order beneath all orders?
And what does it mean to align yourself with it?
The unseen is real.
But our interpretations of it must remain humble.
Subscribe now

title: Morality Is Not Self-Invention
subtitle: What Buddhist ethical training helped me see, and why Islam gave me the constraints contemplation could not produce
text:
There is a kind of morality that is only performance.
It wants to appear good. It wants to be seen as kind, harmless, evolved, spiritual, compassionate, progressive, traditional, pure, wise, or enlightened. It knows the correct language. It knows how to signal virtue. It can sound tender, sophisticated, and sincere.
But beneath that, the system may still be untrained.
The body may still be impulsive. Speech may still be careless. Desire may still be sovereign. The heart may still be full of self-justification. The mind may still be distorted by fantasy, resentment, longing, fear, or pride.
So morality cannot simply mean “being good” in the social sense.
Morality, at its deepest level, is training.
It is the training of the whole human system so that perception, desire, speech, action, relationship, and worship become less distorted. It is not merely about obeying rules in a shallow way. It is about becoming capable of seeing and living truthfully.
This is something Buddhist wisdom traditions often understand with great precision. In Mastering the Core Teachings of the Buddha, Daniel M. Ingram describes morality not as sentimental goodness, but as a practical foundation for clarity. Ethical conduct trains the system. It stabilizes perception. It reduces suffering. It prevents contemplation from being polluted by the consequences of untrained action.
That struck me deeply, because Islam also teaches this, but with a firmer and more complete foundation.
The Buddhist framing sees morality as a technology of liberation: conduct shapes consciousness.
Islam agrees that conduct shapes the soul. But Islam does not leave morality to the self to invent, negotiate, or endlessly reinterpret.
That is the decisive difference.
Because the self is not sovereign.
And if the self is not sovereign, then the self cannot be the final source of morality.

Morality as training, not performance
The passage from Mastering the Core Teachings of the Buddha points toward something important: morality is not simply obedience to a list of rules, and it is not merely social respectability. It is a form of training.
In this framing, morality trains the body, speech, mind, livelihood, relationships, restraint, kindness, honesty, balance, and attention. It is not only about avoiding “bad actions” in an external sense. It is about shaping the conditions under which perception becomes clear.
When a person lies, exploits, indulges impulsively, chases pleasure without restraint, violates boundaries, manipulates others, or lives in contradiction, the mind becomes noisy.
That person may still be intelligent.
They may still be articulate.
They may still be spiritual.
They may still have insights.
They may still speak beautifully about consciousness, healing, love, trauma, liberation, God, or truth.
But the inner instrument becomes distorted.
This is the subtle point:

Immoral action does not only harm the world outside the self. It also damages the self’s ability to see reality clearly.
This is why morality is not separate from contemplation. If the body is acting against truth, if speech is careless, if desire is untrained, if relationships are confused, then contemplation itself becomes polluted.
The person may believe they are seeing deeply, but often what they are seeing is their own untrained nafs projected onto reality.
In that sense, morality is operational spirituality.
It is the daily discipline that makes deeper perception possible.

The Buddhist insight: conduct shapes consciousness
The Buddhist wisdom tradition often frames morality as practical training in reducing suffering.
Ethical conduct stabilizes the system. Restraint reduces agitation. Non-harming reduces karmic consequences. Truthful speech reduces fragmentation. Simplicity reduces craving. Compassion reduces alienation.
This is powerful because it refuses to treat morality as decoration.
Morality is not there so the person can appear noble. It is there because actions have consequences in the nervous system, in relationships, in attention, and in perception.
If one lives in greed, aversion, delusion, fantasy, indulgence, and compulsive self-concern, then the mind becomes trained in those patterns. The person becomes less free.
If one practices restraint, honesty, kindness, patience, generosity, clarity, and steadiness, then the mind becomes more workable. Reality becomes less distorted by reactive patterns.
The Buddhist framing sees morality as a technology of liberation:

Conduct shapes consciousness.
This is very close to what Islam teaches.
But Islam gives the structure a different metaphysical foundation.

Where Islam becomes clearer
Islam also understands that conduct shapes the soul.
But Islam does not leave morality to the self to invent, negotiate, or endlessly reinterpret.
This is the crucial distinction.
In some modern spiritual frameworks, the self becomes the final authority. The person asks:
“What feels aligned?”
“What seems loving?”
“What feels authentic?”
“What produces less suffering according to my current perception?”
Those questions can be useful. They are not meaningless. But they are not enough.
Because the self is not neutral.
The self is not sovereign.
The self is not a pure instrument of truth.
In Islam, the human being is honored, responsible, and capable of moral discernment, but the human being is not the source of morality.
Allah is.
Islam does not rely on the self to produce morality, because the self itself requires purification, discipline, and guidance.
The nafs can justify almost anything when it wants something badly enough.
It can call desire “love.”
It can call fear “wisdom.”
It can call avoidance “peace.”
It can call impulsiveness “authenticity.”
It can call attachment “destiny.”
It can call self-exposure “truth.”
It can call rebellion “freedom.”
It can call spiritual pride “clarity.”
This is why revelation matters.
Islam gives boundaries before the self becomes intoxicated by its own explanations.

What contemplation could see but could not legislate
There is a sharper personal point here.
Thirteen-plus years of contemplation did produce real insight. It was not wasted. It gave me direct, lived recognition that the self is not sovereign, that perception is conditioned, that reality is contingent, and that the “I” cannot honestly pretend to be the absolute center of existence.
That insight matters.
It means contemplation was able to diagnose something true: the self is not ultimate.
The self is unstable, dependent, narrated, affected, wounded, desiring, interpreting, and constantly shaped by conditions it did not create.
But contemplation did not produce the constraints required to live under that truth.
This is the missing bridge.

Contemplation showed that the self is not sovereign, but it could not become the sovereign authority that tells the self how to live.
Contemplation can deconstruct false sovereignty.
It can reveal the emptiness, contingency, instability, and dependence of the self. It can expose egoic narration. It can show how desire distorts perception. It can make the person deeply suspicious of the “I” as final authority.
But after that insight, the embodied question remains:
What do I do with desire?
What do I do with sexuality?
What do I do with anger?
What do I do with money?
What do I do with speech?
What do I do with longing?
What do I do with attention?
What do I do with power?
What do I do with beauty?
What do I do with relationship?
What do I do when I want something intensely?
Who tells me “no” when my own insight is compromised?
This is where contemplation, by itself, reaches a limit.
It can say: “You are not sovereign.”
But it cannot, from within the self alone, generate the binding law that follows from that discovery.
The self cannot discover its non-sovereignty and then crown itself as the lawgiver.
That would be a contradiction.
If the self is contingent, conditioned, and not ultimate, then the self cannot be the final source of moral constraint. It can recognize. It can reason. It can receive. It can submit. It can be trained.
But it cannot be God to itself.
This is why Islam did not merely add “religious content” to an already complete contemplative path. It supplied the missing structure: command, prohibition, ritual, obligation, boundary, accountability, repentance, and submission.
Contemplation diagnosed the problem.
Revelation gave the form of life.
Thirteen years of contemplation taught me that the self is not sovereign; Islam gave me the constraints that follow from that truth.
Or, more directly:

Contemplation deconstructed my false sovereignty, but it could not legislate my life. Revelation did.
This is the clean formulation.
Islam does not negate the contemplative insight. It completes its demand.
If the self is not sovereign, then the next question is not: “How do I become more self-authentic?”
The next question is:
“Who is sovereign, and how do I live under that sovereignty?”
Islam answers: Allah is sovereign.
So the human being does not merely contemplate non-self, contingency, impermanence, dependency, or the instability of ego.
The human being submits to the One who is not contingent.
That is the decisive difference.

Contemplation showed: I am not sovereign.
Islam answered: then live as a servant of the One who is.

The self is not sovereign
The phrase “the self is not sovereign” is central.
In Islam, the human being does not belong to themselves in an absolute sense.
The body is an amānah.
Time is an amānah.
Speech is an amānah.
Beauty is an amānah.
Desire is an amānah.
Intelligence is an amānah.
Relationships are an amānah.
Even spiritual openings are an amānah.
The self is not the owner. The self is entrusted.
This changes everything.
If the self were sovereign, then morality would ultimately be self-legislation: “I decide what is good according to my own inner authority.”
But Islam says the self is a servant, not a god.
The self receives guidance. The self submits. The self is trained into alignment with what is real, not allowed to define reality according to appetite, trauma, fantasy, or mood.
The Islamic moral project is not self-expression.
It is servanthood, purification, and alignment with Allah’s command.
This does not make morality shallow or legalistic.
It makes it safer.
Because without divine boundaries, the ego can hijack spirituality.

Taqwā as moral perception
This connects directly to taqwā.
Taqwā is often translated as God-consciousness, piety, reverence, guardedness, or protective awareness. But it is not merely an emotion. It is a whole-system orientation.
Taqwā means the person lives with awareness that Allah sees.
It means one becomes careful with the body, careful with speech, careful with desire, careful with money, careful with attention, careful with power, careful with emotional influence, careful with other people’s dignity.
Taqwā is not fear in the crude sense.
It is protective consciousness.
It protects the soul from heedlessness.
It protects relationships from injustice.
It protects worship from hypocrisy.
It protects love from becoming possession.
It protects desire from becoming intoxication.
It protects intelligence from becoming arrogance.
It protects softness from becoming boundarylessness.
It protects spirituality from becoming self-worship.
Taqwā is morality with Allah at the center.
The Buddhist framing might say:
“Act ethically because unethical action destabilizes perception and produces suffering.”
Islam can affirm that, but it adds:
Act ethically because Allah is real.
Because you are accountable.
Because the self is not divine.
Because the soul is shaped by obedience or disobedience.
Because true freedom is found in submission to the Real.

Tazkiyah: purification of the inner system
This also connects to tazkiyah, the purification and growth of the soul.
Tazkiyah is not a vague spiritual aesthetic. It is not simply feeling pure, soft, poetic, or emotionally elevated. It is the disciplined purification of the nafs so that the person becomes more truthful, more humble, more restrained, more grateful, more patient, more merciful, and more obedient to Allah.
Tazkiyah includes many of the same qualities named in Buddhist ethical training:
kindness toward oneself and others.
Honesty.
Respect for boundaries.
Skillful support of oneself.
Skillful support of others.
Gratitude.
Patience.
Persistence.
Clarity.
Integrity.
Dedication to service.
Restraint.
Non-harming.
Balance.
Right livelihood.
Careful speech.
Careful relationship.
But Islam grounds these not merely in psychological wellbeing or reduced suffering.
Islam grounds them in the reality of Allah, the guidance of revelation, the accountability of the ākhirah, and the purification of the heart.
Tazkiyah is not the self perfecting itself by itself.
It is the servant being purified through guidance, struggle, repentance, worship, and mercy.
This matters because the self cannot always diagnose itself accurately.
Sometimes the self needs a command.
Sometimes it needs a prohibition.
Sometimes it needs a limit.
Sometimes it needs to be stopped before it can produce a beautiful explanation for its own corruption.

Why boundaries are mercy
This is where Islam feels clearer than many self-generated moral systems.
Islam gives boundaries around desire, money, sex, speech, food, anger, marriage, gender interaction, business, worship, family, public conduct, privacy, and power.
From the outside, the nafs may experience this as restriction.
But spiritually, these boundaries are mercy.
They protect the human being from having to reinvent morality every time the self becomes activated.
When desire rises, the boundary is already there.
When anger rises, the boundary is already there.
When attraction rises, the boundary is already there.
When ambition rises, the boundary is already there.
When sadness rises, the boundary is already there.
When the ego wants revenge, exposure, validation, fantasy, dominance, seduction, or escape, the boundary is already there.
A divine boundary is not a cage for the soul.
It is a guardrail against the nafs when the nafs is least trustworthy.
This is why Islam does not say “follow your heart” in the modern sentimental sense.
The heart can be purified, yes.
But the heart can also be diseased.
The nafs can whisper.
Shayṭān can beautify.
Trauma can distort.
Desire can intoxicate.
Social approval can corrupt.
Loneliness can make almost anything feel meaningful.
So Islam gives a structure that remains stable even when the inner weather changes.

Morality and perception
The deeper point is that morality and perception are linked.
A person does not simply “see reality” as they are.
They see through the condition of the heart.
If the heart is trained in truthfulness, restraint, remembrance, gratitude, patience, and humility, perception becomes clearer.
If the heart is trained in self-justification, indulgence, envy, resentment, fantasy, performance, and heedlessness, perception becomes darker.
This is why sin is not only a legal category.
It is also perceptual damage.
It leaves marks.
It changes taste.
It changes what feels normal.
It changes what one is willing to tolerate.
It changes what one calls love.
It changes what one calls freedom.
It changes what one calls spirituality.
Every repeated action trains perception.
The question is: trained toward Allah, or trained toward the nafs?
This is where Buddhist moral training and Islamic tazkiyah strongly overlap. Both understand that repeated conduct shapes the field of experience.
But Islam adds a more explicit vertical axis.
The purpose is not merely clarity, calm, or liberation from suffering.
The purpose is knowing, worshipping, obeying, and returning to Allah.

The danger of “positive” and “negative” without revelation
The passage’s warning that “negative” and “positive” action can become arbitrary is important.
Human beings often define good and bad according to culture, mood, convenience, ideology, trauma, or group belonging.
One society praises what another condemns.
One subculture calls something healing while another calls it selfish.
One person calls something honest while another experiences it as cruelty.
One spiritual group calls something liberated while another sees it as heedless.
Without a deeper criterion, morality becomes unstable.
Islam’s answer is not that human reason is useless. Islam honors reason. But reason is not left alone as the final authority.
Reason is guided by revelation.
The fitrah recognizes truth, but the fitrah can be covered.
The heart can know, but the heart can also be diseased.
The intellect can discern, but the intellect can also rationalize.
Revelation does not erase moral intelligence.
It protects moral intelligence from being enslaved by the nafs.
This is the key difference.
Islam does not ask the human being to abandon discernment.
It asks the human being to place discernment under Allah.

Desire is not the enemy
This matters especially when desire is involved.
The issue is not that desire exists.
Desire is part of the human design.
Attraction is not automatically corruption.
Softness is not automatically weakness.
Longing is not automatically misguidance.
The wish for marriage, intimacy, tenderness, protection, children, beauty, and companionship is not inherently dirty.
Islam does not require hatred of embodiment.
Islam does not teach escape from being human.
But desire must be trained.
Desire must be placed inside taqwā.
Desire must be tested by time, boundaries, character, prayer, counsel, and reality.
Desire cannot be allowed to crown itself as revelation.
Desire is not the enemy.
Untrained desire is the danger.
The nafs can take a real human longing and inflate it into a private mythology.
It can turn a person into a symbol.
It can turn attraction into destiny.
It can turn emotional intensity into certainty.
It can turn longing into spiritual interpretation.
It can turn softness into surrender before there is any real trustworthiness.
So the test is not simply: “Do I feel something?”
The test is:
Does this feeling remain within the boundaries of Allah?
Does it increase prayer or distract from prayer?
Does it produce clarity or intoxication?
Does it make me more truthful or more performative?
Does it protect dignity or expose dignity?
Does it respect reality or feed fantasy?
Does it move slowly enough for character to be revealed?
Does it make me more obedient or more self-justifying?
Does it strengthen taqwā or weaken it?

Can you remain morally trained while emotionally activated?
This is the central practical question.
Not: “Is this person good?”
Not: “Am I attracted?”
Not: “Is this destiny?”
Not: “Is this a sign?”
Not: “Can I make a beautiful interpretation out of this?”
The real question is:
Can you remain morally trained while emotionally activated?
That means keeping boundaries even when the heart becomes soft.
Speaking truthfully even when fantasy would feel sweeter.
Avoiding emotional intoxication.
Not using spirituality to justify attachment.
Keeping ṣalāh central.
Protecting your dignity.
Protecting the other person’s dignity.
Refusing public performance of private longing.
Refusing to turn a human being into a spiritual object.
Moving slowly enough for reality to disclose itself.
Staying obedient when desire wants exceptions.
Letting Allah reveal, with time, what is real and what is projection.
This is morality as training.
This is taqwā under activation.
This is where the theory becomes real.

Islam, Buddhism, and the architecture of moral training
There is a shared architecture between Buddhist ethical training and Islamic purification.
Both recognize that conduct shapes perception.
Both understand that desire can distort reality.
Both recognize that restraint is not merely repression, but protection.
Both see that harmful action harms the one who acts.
Both understand that morality is not just outward behavior, but inward training.
But the foundations are not identical.
Buddhist moral training often begins from suffering, delusion, craving, and the instability of the self. It trains ethical conduct, restraint, compassion, and mindfulness so that perception becomes clearer and suffering is reduced.
Islam begins from Allah.
It understands the human being as created, entrusted, tested, accountable, and in need of guidance. It trains the soul through taqwā, tazkiyah, adab, worship, obedience, repentance, and revealed boundaries.
Buddhist moral training can show how ethics stabilizes consciousness.
Islam shows why consciousness itself must submit to Allah.
That distinction matters.
Because if consciousness is not sovereign, then clarity is not enough.
The clear mind still needs guidance.
The insightful self still needs boundaries.
The contemplative person still needs revelation.

The problem with relying on the self alone
If morality depends only on what the self can perceive, then morality becomes vulnerable whenever perception is compromised.
And perception is often compromised precisely when morality is most needed.
When a person is angry, they see selectively.
When a person is attracted, they idealize.
When a person is hurt, they distort.
When a person is lonely, they attach meaning too quickly.
When a person is praised, they inflate.
When a person is rejected, they collapse.
When a person is spiritually excited, they may mistake intensity for guidance.
When a person is intellectually powerful, they may mistake explanation for truth.
This is why the self cannot be the final judge.
The nafs is most dangerous when it can speak in spiritual language.
It can quote wisdom while avoiding obedience.
It can speak of love while violating boundaries.
It can speak of authenticity while indulging impulse.
It can speak of healing while refusing accountability.
It can speak of intuition while ignoring revelation.
It can speak of freedom while becoming enslaved to appetite.
Islam interrupts this with command, prohibition, worship, repentance, and accountability.
Not to humiliate the human being.
But to save the human being from self-deception.

Adab: the form of purified morality
Another Islamic word that belongs here is adab.
Adab is often translated as manners, but it is much deeper than politeness. Adab is rightful placement. It means putting things where they belong.
Allah is not placed beneath the self.
Desire is not placed above revelation.
A person is not placed in the position of Lord.
Marriage is not placed above obedience.
Public speech is not placed above dignity.
Emotional intensity is not placed above truth.
Spiritual language is not placed above action.
The body is not treated as an enemy.
The nafs is not treated as a god.
Adab is the moral architecture of reality: everything in its proper place before Allah.
This is why adab is so stabilizing.
It prevents the inner world from becoming a monarchy of mood.
It teaches the person to relate correctly: to Allah, to the self, to others, to knowledge, to desire, to pain, to love, and to limits.

The Islamic correction to spiritual bypass
Do not use spirituality to escape embodiment.
Use revelation to guide embodiment.
Islam is not asking for dissociation from the body. It is not asking for hatred of desire. It is not asking for coldness, numbness, or performative purity.
It is asking for guidance.
Embodiment needs guidance.
Desire needs guidance.
Love needs guidance.
Anger needs guidance.
Speech needs guidance.
Beauty needs guidance.
Power needs guidance.
Tenderness needs guidance.
Without guidance, even beautiful things become dangerous.
Islam does not erase embodiment.
It disciplines embodiment so the body can serve Allah rather than enslave the soul.
This is a much more precise frame than “desire bad” or “desire good.”
Desire is a force.
Like fire, it can warm or burn.
It depends on placement, container, intention, and boundary.

Practical integration
The integration is not abstract.
When emotionally activated, the work is not to suppress every feeling.
The work is to place every feeling under Allah.
Return to ṣalāh before interpretation.
If a feeling makes prayer harder, that is information.
Do not rush meaning.
Let reality unfold before naming something destiny, sign, love, or danger.
Keep speech clean.
No exaggeration. No seductive ambiguity. No unnecessary exposure. No public emotional performance.
Protect dignity.
Your dignity and the other person’s dignity are both part of taqwā.
Test by boundaries.
What cannot survive a halal boundary is not love. It is appetite, fantasy, or dependency.
Watch the nervous system.
If something produces agitation, obsession, compulsive checking, emotional intoxication, or prayerlessness, slow down.
Prefer reality over symbolism.
Symbols can illuminate, but they can also intoxicate. Character is revealed through action over time.
Let Allah be the criterion.
Not chemistry.
Not loneliness.
Not fantasy.
Not aesthetic resonance.
Not intellectual compatibility alone.
The sign of healthy desire is not intensity.
It is whether the desire can remain obedient, patient, truthful, and dignified.

The deeper synthesis
The deeper synthesis is this:
Buddhist moral training correctly sees that unwholesome action clouds perception and increases suffering. It understands that morality is not arbitrary social performance but a practical discipline that shapes consciousness.
Islam affirms that actions shape the heart, but gives a clearer anchor.
Allah is the source of moral reality.
Revelation gives boundaries.
The Prophet ﷺ shows embodied guidance.
The self must be purified rather than obeyed as sovereign.
In Buddhist language, morality stabilizes the system.
In Islamic language, taqwā protects the soul.
In cybernetic language, ethical conduct purifies the feedback loops.
In Qur’anic language, the human being must not follow desire as if desire were a lord.
The shared architecture is clear:
Conduct shapes perception.
The Islamic completion is clearer:
Perception itself must be submitted to Allah.

This book still belongs in my system, but not as an authority above Islam.
It belongs as a comparative mirror.
It can help name certain mechanics: how action shapes consciousness, how restraint reduces suffering, how morality stabilizes perception, how harmful conduct creates noise.
But Islam gives the criterion.
The boundary.
The vertical axis.
The final authority.
So the takeaway is this:
Do not use spirituality to escape embodiment.
Use revelation to guide embodiment.
Desire is not the enemy.
Untrained desire is the danger.
Softness is not the enemy.
Boundaryless attachment is the danger.
Love is not the enemy.
Projection is the danger.
The self is not sovereign.
Allah is.
Morality is not self-invention.
It is purification under guidance.
Before, this was contemplation.
Now it is integration.
The question is no longer only whether a teaching is beautiful.
The question is whether it trains the soul into truth.
And in Islam, truth is not whatever the self can elegantly explain.
Truth is what aligns the servant with Allah.

title: The World Is Not Facing One Crisis, It Is Facing a Crisis of Orientation
text:
Photo by Issy Bailey on Unsplash
There is a temptation, when looking at the world, to search for the crisis.
One explanation.
One enemy.
One event that explains why everything feels unstable.
But the more carefully we look, the more obvious it becomes that the dominant pattern is not one single crisis. It is multiple interacting pressures, geopolitical, ecological, technological, economic, psychological, and spiritual, feeding back into one another.
The world is not simply “falling apart.” It is revealing what happens when complex systems lose orientation.
And perhaps, more deeply, it is revealing what happens when human beings orient themselves around created things as if those things were ultimate.

A world of interacting pressures
Modern life is often described in categories: politics, climate, economy, technology, health, religion, psychology. We separate these domains because it helps us think.
But reality does not separate them so cleanly.
Energy affects geopolitics.
Geopolitics affects food systems.
Food systems affect migration.
Migration affects politics.
Technology affects attention.
Attention affects trust.
Trust affects institutions.
Institutions affect whether societies can respond to crisis at all.
This is why the present moment can feel so difficult to understand. We are not watching isolated problems. We are watching systems interact.
And when systems interact under pressure, small failures can cascade.
A disruption in energy infrastructure is not only an energy story. It becomes an economic story, a military story, a domestic political story, and eventually a psychological story. A climate event is not only an environmental event. It becomes a food story, a migration story, a debt story, and a governance story.
The deeper question is not only: What is happening?
It is: What kind of orientation allows human beings to remain truthful, humble, and responsible inside a world this complex?

Geopolitics: conflict has moved into infrastructure
One of the clearest features of our age is that modern conflict increasingly targets the systems beneath everyday life.
Wars are no longer only fought at front lines. They are fought through logistics, fuel supplies, ports, pipelines, electricity grids, satellites, information networks, and financial systems.
Energy infrastructure has become a battlefield because energy is the hidden bloodstream of modern civilization.
Oil terminals.
Fuel supplies.
Electricity.
Transport.
These are not background details. They are the conditions that allow hospitals, food distribution, heating, communication, and military operations to function.
Vaclav Smil has spent much of his work reminding us that civilization is not an abstraction. It is built on continuous material flows: energy, food, water, metals, fertilizers, transportation networks. When those flows are stable, modern people can forget they exist. When they are disrupted, everyone remembers at once.
This is part of the strange fragility of the modern world. We live inside systems that feel permanent precisely because they usually work.
Until they do not.

Climate and Earth systems: the planet is not passive
At the same time, the physical Earth continues to remind us that it is not a passive stage on which human history unfolds.
Volcanoes erupt.
Earthquakes occur.
Weather patterns shift.
Water systems become stressed.
Ecosystems change.
Not every eruption, earthquake, flood, or heatwave is evidence of something unprecedented. The Earth has always been dynamic. But climate change adds another layer: it increases stress across systems that human beings already depend upon.
Water resources.
Agriculture.
Food security.
Coastal settlements.
Biodiversity.
Public health.
Ecological pressure often unfolds slowly, which makes it easy to ignore. Human attention is drawn to sudden shocks. But some of the most important transformations happen gradually, over years and decades, until the new condition becomes undeniable.
This is one of the great challenges of our time: the most consequential problems are not always the loudest.
They do not always announce themselves with a single dramatic event. Sometimes they arrive through trends, thresholds, and delayed feedback.
And delayed feedback is dangerous, because by the time a system finally corrects us, the correction can be severe.

Technology: AI is becoming infrastructure
Artificial intelligence is no longer only a novelty or a toy for experimentation. It is moving toward infrastructure.
That changes the question.
The question is no longer simply, “Can AI do this?”
Increasingly, the question is:
How should societies integrate AI responsibly?
Into healthcare.
Into education.
Into administration.
Into creative work.
Into decision-making.
Into war.
Into intimacy.
Into the formation of attention itself.
Technology does not merely give us tools. It reshapes what we notice, what we reward, what we outsource, and what we become comfortable not knowing.
This is why AI cannot be understood only as a productivity tool. It is also a governance question, a labor question, a moral question, and a spiritual question.
If human beings use powerful tools without wisdom, the tools amplify whatever orientation already governs the heart.
A tool in the hands of arrogance amplifies arrogance.
A tool in the hands of greed amplifies extraction.
A tool in the hands of fear amplifies control.
A tool in the hands of humility can serve mercy, knowledge, and repair.
The technology matters.
But the orientation of the user matters more.

The quiet crisis: ecology
Ecology may be one of the quietest stories and one of the most important.
Biodiversity loss, water stress, soil degradation, agricultural adaptation, and climate pressure do not always fit the tempo of the news cycle. They unfold too slowly for constant attention and too broadly for easy blame.
But ecological decline is not a separate issue from human life. It is the condition underneath human life.
The Qur’an repeatedly invites human beings to look at creation not as a dead resource field, but as a sign. The heavens, the earth, rain, plants, animals, alternation of night and day, these are not spiritually neutral decorations. They are reminders.
Creation is not God.
But creation points beyond itself.
A spiritually disoriented civilization can make two opposite mistakes. It can worship nature as ultimate, or it can treat nature as meaningless material. Both are errors.
The healthier orientation is neither idolization nor exploitation.
It is trust, stewardship, humility, and gratitude.
To care for creation without worshipping it.
To benefit from creation without imagining we own it absolutely.
To study creation without forgetting the Creator.

Human psychology may be the most important layer
Beneath geopolitics, economics, technology, and ecology is another layer: human psychology.
This may be the most important layer of all.
Across societies we can observe similar patterns:

polarization,

information overload,

algorithmic amplification,

identity conflict,

distrust,

resentment,

exhaustion,

moral confusion.
These are not only political problems. They are feedback loops inside human cognition.
A society cannot respond wisely to crisis if its attention is fragmented, its trust is collapsing, and its people are constantly being trained to react rather than reflect.
Modern information systems do not simply inform us. They shape us. They reward speed, outrage, certainty, performance, and tribal belonging. They make it easy to confuse stimulation with understanding.
And when people are overwhelmed, they often do not become wiser. They become more reactive.
This matters because most global problems are not mysteries.
We know that war destroys societies.
We know corruption weakens institutions.
We know ecosystems have limits.
We know debt accumulates.
We know hatred escalates.
We know energy systems are vulnerable.
We know human beings are not psychologically built for endless comparison, outrage, and stimulation.
The limiting factor is often not information.
It is orientation.

The spiritual diagnosis: misorientation
From a spiritual perspective, the central problem is that human beings repeatedly orient themselves around created things as if they were ultimate.
Power.
Wealth.
Nation.
Desire.
Status.
Knowledge.
Technology.
The self.
None of these things are necessarily evil in themselves. The problem begins when something created is treated as ultimate — when it becomes the center around which the heart, society, and imagination revolve.
The Qur’an names this pattern again and again.
It speaks of the human being who thinks they are self-sufficient:

“No! Indeed, man transgresses because he sees himself as self-sufficient.”
— Qur’an 96:6–7
It speaks of the one who takes desire as a god:

“Have you seen the one who takes as his god his own desire?”
— Qur’an 25:43
It speaks of those who forget Allah and therefore lose themselves:

“And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves.”
— Qur’an 59:19
The problem is not lack of intelligence.
It is misorientation.
The Torah gives a similar warning through the repeated command to remember: remember who brought you out of Egypt, do not forget when you become prosperous, do not make idols, do not trust your own strength alone.
Jesus, too, repeatedly returns to orientation:
Where is your treasure?
Whom do you serve?
What do you seek first?
Again and again, the question is not only what humans know, but what they love, trust, worship, and obey.

Systems drift and spiritual forgetfulness
In systems language, every adaptive system needs a reference.
A thermostat needs a setpoint.
An airplane needs navigation.
A cell needs regulatory networks.
A society needs shared meaning.
A human being needs an ultimate orientation.
Without a stable reference, a system begins correcting against its own previous outputs. Error accumulates. Drift increases.
Human beings are not merely engineering systems, of course. We are moral and spiritual beings. We do not only process information. We choose what we serve.
This is where systems thinking and revelation begin to speak to one another.
Systems thinking helps us see feedback, delay, dependence, fragility, and unintended consequences.
Revelation tells us the deeper truth: the human being is dependent upon Allah whether they acknowledge it or not.
There are two intertwined realities here:

Ontological dependence — we are always dependent upon Allah.

Moral orientation — we are responsible for whether we acknowledge and live according to that dependence.
The first is always true.
The second is where accountability enters.
A person can be dependent and still ungrateful.
A society can be sustained and still arrogant.
A civilization can be given abundance and still forget.
And when forgetfulness becomes collective, drift becomes structural.

The illusion of independence
One of the most dangerous effects of prosperity is that it hides dependence.
When systems work, we begin to believe we are self-sufficient.
Food appears in stores.
Water comes from taps.
Electricity flows.
Hospitals function.
Phones connect.
Supply chains deliver.
Institutions maintain order.
The more reliable these systems become, the easier it is to forget the layers of dependence beneath them.
Dependence on farmers, drivers, nurses, engineers, sanitation workers, teachers, caregivers, and countless unseen hands.
Dependence on soil, rain, sunlight, pollinators, minerals, oceans, forests, and atmospheric stability.
And beneath all of that, dependence upon Allah.
Success can create the illusion of independence.
Surplus can delay feedback.
Delayed feedback can allow arrogance to grow.
And reality eventually corrects us.
This is not only a political observation. It is a spiritual one.
The human being drifts not only because they do not know, but because they forget what their knowledge should produce: humility, gratitude, justice, restraint, and worship.

So what is the correct orientation?
If I had to summarize the Qur’anic answer, I would say:

Remember Allah.

Worship Allah alone.

Seek truth.

Act justly.

Be grateful.

Be humble.

Care for creation without worshipping it.

Recognize that everything created is contingent and passing.
None of this requires denying science.
None of this requires rejecting systems thinking.
None of this requires becoming passive in the face of worldly problems.
It requires putting every created thing — including our own minds — in its proper place.
Science can describe patterns in creation.
Systems thinking can help us understand interdependence.
Medicine can heal by Allah’s permission.
Technology can serve human needs.
Politics can restrain harm and pursue justice.
But none of these can become ultimate.
When they do, they become idols — not always in the form of statues, but in the form of total trust, total fear, total obedience, or total identity.
The question is not whether we participate in the world. We must.
The question is whether we participate with remembrance or forgetfulness.

Local responsibility in a global age
The world is too large for any one person to control.
That sentence can feel frightening, but it can also be a mercy.
We are not asked to hold the entire world in our hands. We are asked to respond faithfully to what has been placed before us.
To pray.
To study.
To tell the truth.
To care for the people entrusted to us.
To do the work in front of us with excellence.
To notice small signs of beauty.
To resist despair.
To refuse arrogance.
To return, again and again, to Allah.
Global awareness is useful, but it can become spiritually harmful if it replaces local responsibility. It is possible to become emotionally consumed by distant crises while neglecting the obligations directly in front of us.
The point is not to look away from the world.
The point is to look at the world from the right center.
If our attention is grounded in reality and our orientation is toward Allah, then we can study the whole system without being consumed by it.
We can see instability without worshipping stability.
We can see collapse without becoming addicted to collapse.
We can see suffering without losing trust.
We can see complexity without pretending to be God.

From explanation to wisdom
At first, the question may be:
How does reality work?
That is a necessary question. It leads to study, observation, science, history, and systems thinking.
But eventually another question becomes more important:
How should I be oriented within reality?
The first question seeks explanation.
The second seeks wisdom.
We need both. But explanation without wisdom can become another form of power. It can feed the ego. It can make us feel above others because we “see the system.”
Wisdom does something different.
Wisdom makes knowledge bow.
It turns perception into humility.
It turns awareness into responsibility.
It turns fear into prayer.
It turns complexity into worship.
The world is not facing one crisis. It is facing many interacting crises. But underneath them is an older and deeper human problem: we forget our dependence, mistake created things for ultimate things, and then wonder why our systems drift.
The response is not denial.
The response is return.
To Allah.
To truth.
To gratitude.
To justice.
To humility.
To the responsibilities directly in front of us.
Because the world may be too large for us to control, but it is not too large for us to remain truthful within it.
And sometimes, in an age of noise, that itself is a form of resistance.
